Looking at the `urllib3` code, this seems to be caused by support for HTTP 0.9 "simple responses" in `httplib`. There is a `strict` parameter to `httplib.HTTPConnection` which when set will cause a `BadStatusLine` to be raised for these cases.

The `strict` parameter and support for "simple responses" was removed in version 3.4 of `httplib`, so retesting this with a Python 3 virtualenv, I do get a `BadStatusLine` exception for status lines like these.

## Sample

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python

import cv2

import scipy.misc
import numpy as np
import imgaug
import imgaug.augmenters as iaa


seq = iaa.Sequential([
    iaa.ChangeColorspace(to_colorspace='HSV', alpha=1, from_colorspace='RGB'),
    iaa.Add(value=[-50, 50], per_channel=True, channels=[2]),
    iaa.Add(value=[-10, 10], per_channel=True, channels=[1]),
    iaa.ChangeColorspace(to_colorspace='RGB', alpha=1, from_colorspace='HSV'),
],
random_order=False)


i = 0
while True:
    img = scipy.misc.imread('quokka.jpg')
    img_aug = seq.augment_image(img)

    viz = np.hstack((img, img_aug))

    cv2.imshow('spam', viz[:, :, ::-1])
    scipy.misc.imsave('out/%04d.jpg' % i, viz)
    i += 1
    key = cv2.waitKey(0)
    if key == ord('q'):
        break
```

Here is a time comparison I did between the three warp affine methods: scikit-image, Pillow, and opencv. 
Note the y-axis is on a log scale, so a minor change in slop actually means quite a lot.  

The results show that opencv is a clear winner in terms of speed. Pillow does pretty well, note that this benchmark includes conversion to and from numpy. skimage performs the worst by a significant margin. 

However, each method does have its merits. skimage gives you access to several options that doesn't exist in opencv or pillow. Likewise opencv gives you lanczos resampling, which pil and skimage do not have. PIL is definately the most restricted in terms of its api. The only border mode is constant (I think there is a PR to let you set the fill value right now, but by default it can't do that), and it can't only will accept multichannel images in uint8 format (although you can use anything for grayscale).

I think the best approach is to have a backend option that allows the user to specify pil, cv2, or skimage when performing affine transforms.

Yes, it is surprising, but IMHO it is a feature of pint: it allows you to create and operate with quantities whose magnitude is of an arbitrary type (it essentially allows [Duck Typing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Duck_typing)). 

For example, one can create a pint quantity using a [Decimal](https://docs.python.org/2/library/decimal.html) instance for the magnitude, and pint behaves as expected out-of-the-box:

```python
import pint
from decimal import Decimal
ureg = pint.UnitRegistry()
a = Decimal('0.1') * ureg.meter
b = Decimal('0.3') * ureg.meter
assert not bool(a + a + a - b ) 
```
I am not against changing this in Pint as long as we do not disrupt existing users.

This pull request fixes a bug in the _get_user_dir_path method that created a
directory named '~' in the current working directory unless either of
the following were true:

1. the legacy user config directory, named $HOME/.thefuck, existed
2. the environment variable XDG_CONFIG_HOME existed and contained /fully expanded paths (i.e. $HOME/.config instead of ~/.config)

The simplest change to fix the issue would be to change line 52 of
thefuck/conf.py as so:
    `--- return Path(xdg_config_dir).joinpath('thefuck')`
    `+++ return Path(xdg_config_dir).expanduser().joinpath('thefuck')`

However, this changeset includes some minor refactoring to provide:
- a single 'return value' statement
- fixes the logging statement for the case where XDG_CONFIG_HOME is set to something other than $HOME/.config

I think that you should *not* mess with user specified `cflags`. These should be provided last so that the user can override anything else being set by FoBiS.py. Most compilers respect that convention and if a user is explicitly passing her own `-cflags` then she knows what she is doing… and she may be intentionally trying to override what FoBiS.py thinks is the right thing to do. FoBiS.py should pass these user `cflags` along to the compiler faithfully, and at the end of the compile command. A user may be able to gain insight by passing `coverage` and `cflags ' --O2'` if they’re trying to figure out some strange behavior like a compiler bug or want more insight on the difference between the optimized code and the unoptimized code.

If you want to run a regexp against the default or FoBiS.py inserted cflags, something simple like what you have or `-O[0-3s]? ` should work. But please don’t edit user `cflags`! Life is indeed too short to try to support every single compiler flag

The restriction seems arbitrary, but the reason for its existence is to match the behavior of the variable constructor with the boolean expression parser.

So the code from line 94 of `pyeda/boolalg/boolfunc.py`:

```python
        if not re.match(r"^[^\d\W]\w*$", name, flags=re.UNICODE):
            fstr = "expected name to match (letter|'_')(letter|digit|'_')*, got {}"
            raise ValueError(fstr.format(name))
```

matches the code from line 187 of `pyeda/parsing/boolexpr.py`:

```python
            (r"[a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_]*", name),
```

(this is an entry in a lexical analysis table).

That said, it's true you could just comment out lines 94-96 of `pyeda/parsing/boolexpr.py`, and the engine doesn't really care what you name your variables since the internal representation only preserves a mapping from the name to the variable object.

Recently I've been hacking on a symbolic boolean algebra library in C++, [boolexpr](https://github.com/cjdrake/boolexpr). In that library, I made different decision about how variables names work. You have to define a `Context`, which is sort of a local namespace for variables, and you can name whatever you want. This wasn't because it mattered to me that people want to name variables weird things like `a<b`, but rather that there are an abundance of transformations (such as Tseytin) that add auxiliary variables to expressions, and there needs to be an easy way to organize them.

Anyways, kind of a long blurb. I'm okay with removing the variable name restriction, since it doesn't really harm anything. Feel free to submit a pull request.

You may have a look at my fork of mlxtend. Since this is just for first demonstration purposes [neither test, nor other of you styleguides ](https://rasbt.github.io/mlxtend/contributing/)are in place.

The `generate_rules()` method takes basically three arguments:

1. _dataframe_ containing the frequent itemsets
2. _metric_ to decide whether or not a rule is considered to be of interest
3. _minimal threshold_ that determines the value range of the metric for which you would consider a rule interesting

Based on those parameters candidates are generated from the frequent itemsets and evaluated.

A first description can be found in [association_rules.ipynb](https://github.com/JoshuaGoerner/mlxtend/blob/rule_generation/docs/sources/user_guide/association/association_rules.ipynb) here.

The source code for the rule generation can be found in [association_rules.py](https://github.com/JoshuaGoerner/mlxtend/blob/rule_generation/mlxtend/association/association_rules.py).

First of all, if you are trying to subclass `BaseLaTexContainer` and you want to let your `__init__` do nothing different from its `__init__` you shouldn't define one, since it will just use the `__init__` from `BaseLaTexContainer`. If you need to do something yourself as well, you should just do that and pass data and packages to `super().__init__(data=data, packages=packages). You could even leave packages out if you don't need it.

Lastly, never use a list as a default value, since it is mutable and it causes weird and unwanted behaviour. See: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/1132941/least-astonishment-in-python-the-mutable-default-argument

I think starting with a pytest suite is the way to go. Since one has to implement quite a few python scripts anyway to do the kind of testing you'd like to see. The overhead of writing a pytest instead of an manual/interactive test session is relatively low.

But writing a test (whether in pytest or by doing manual runs) for all modules is a lot of work. So my questions are:

* how would you prioritize the testing of each module?
* which modules are not well covered by the examples? i thought about running the examples using gcov and then start from the least covered core-interfaces.
* do you agree that the main goal of the testing in this PR is "to not break existing dlib python scripts in the wild"? this implies that the tests should be written against the boost::python interface first.

Hi, thanks for posting the question. The problem is the syntax for context references in expression rvalues is super confusing. 

```python
from durable.lang import *

with ruleset('a8'):
    @when_all(c.first << s.foo == True, 
              c.second << m.amount < c.s.max_amount + c.s.ref_id('global').min_amount)
    def approved(c):
        print ('a8 approved {0}'.format(c.second.amount))

    @when_start
    def start(host):
        sid = 10001
        id = 10001
        host.patch_state('a8', {'sid': sid, 'foo': False, 'max_amount': 500})
        host.patch_state('a8', {'sid': 'global', 'min_amount': 100})
        host.post('a8', {'id': id, 'sid': sid, 'amount': 10})

run_all()
```

The rule above expects two events: 1. A change in the context state, setting the property `foo` to value `True` 2. A message with the `amount` property less than the current context state `max_amount` plus the global context state `min_amount`. Please note how comparing a change in the context state uses different syntax than referencing the local context state.

@b takes an integer number of days, not a period string. It causes the begin by notice to show on the current day, all day long, whenever the current day is @b or fewer days from the date of the task or event. E.g., the beginby @b 3d with @s 10/15 10pm would display a begin by notice all day long on 10/12, 10/13 and 10/14

In contrast, the alert @a 3d with @s 10/15 10pm would trigger the alert once at 10pm on 10/12.

Now suppose to the contrary that @b accepted period strings. Shouldn't the effect of @b 3d10h in a task with a starting time of 8am be the same as @b 4d?  And shouldn’t the effect of @b 4h30m be the same as not having an entry for @b at all? Somehow “an integer number of days before the date of the item” seems less confusing to me than “a period string which, taking account of the starting time of the item, will be converted to an integer number of days before the date of the item”.

I've finally gotten around to actually writing some [documentation](https://github.com/mikf/gallery-dl/blob/master/docs/configuration.rst#extractor-options) that should make the use of categories and subcategories more obvious and hopefully addresses some of the issues you mentioned.
Now to answer some of your points:

> Here's what is happening, to my understanding
> 1. ...
> 2. ...

It's actually simpler than that: Each extractor class has a list of regular expressions (usually containing only 1) which match the whole URL. A match is found by going through all regexps from all extractor classes and applying the regular expressions until one matches the URL. The [upper level function](https://github.com/mikf/gallery-dl/blob/master/gallery_dl/extractor/__init__.py#L81-L87) for that is only 5 lines of actual code.
There is no difference in how the choice between two entirely different extractors or between two related (sub-)extractors is made.

> Furthermore, all extractors and variants should be properly documented somewhere
> ...

On Linux it's a lot easier than on Windows which sucks for these kind of things. To say one, you can simply use SSH to send commands to the remote host which will have a python script which collects some metrics via psutil, serialize them by using JSON and print the serialized string to stdout. The stdout will be piped through, returned back to you, you deserialize it and you're done.
For example, something like this will print the content of remote /tmp directory:

    ssh -o "StrictHostKeyChecking no" user@192.168.1.7 "ls /tmp"

Replace "ls /tmp" with "python /path/to/your/script.py" and you're done.
If you don't want to type the password every time simply use authentication based on SSH keys.
Reimplementing something like this (SSH) which provides transport, authentication, encryption and a tons of other things into psutil is out of question: it's a totally different project.
Let's have the SSH guys take care of those things and psutil take care of its things. =)

Yes all values are supposed to be expressed in bytes.
This is how it's done on FreeBSD:
https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/master/psutil/arch/freebsd/specific.c#L501
The C type is an `int`, which indeed makes me thing we're not dealing with bytes (`int` is too small).
I searched a bit and in here:
https://github.com/KDE/ksysguard/blob/master/ksysguardd/FreeBSD/Memory.c#L159
...they convert it as `swap_total * pagesize / 1024`.

`DelegatorBot` in the traditional version is using multiple threads to handle multiple users (one thread per user). As a developer, you don't need to manage the threads yourself. Telepot takes care of that for you. If traffic is not too high, this approach is enough. I encourage beginners/students to use the traditional version. Async version is really for advanced developers.

reviewing the code, i think this is kinda flawed. I think the proper pipeline should be something like:

```
socket loop => route packets into decoder based upon ssrc => decoder does buffering and outputs PCM audio after a delay (poor man's jitter buffer). 
```

We can use a static delay of a reasonable time since we don't need real time decoding.

```
## Socket loop:
decoders = defaultdict(Decoder)
while True:
    packet = receive_packet()
    rtp_packet = decode_rtp_packet(packet)
    # Packet isn't an rtp packet!
    if not rtp_packet:
        continue
    
    decoders[rtp_packet.ssrc].feed(rtp_packet)
```

Then something like:

```py
class Decoder:
    def __init__(self, window_delay=2):
        self.current_packets = []
        self.next_packets = []
        self.start_timestamp = None
        self.end_timestamp = None
        self.swap_timestamp = None
        self.window_delay = window_delay
        self.decoder = make_decoder()

    def feed(self, rtp_packet):
        cur_ts = rtp_packet.timestamp
        if self.end_timestamp is None:
            self.start_timestamp = cur_ts
            self.end_timestamp = cur_ts + self.window_delay
            call_in_the_future(cur_ts + (self.window_delay * 2), self.flush_packets)
            self.current_packets.append(rtp_packet)
            return

        # The packet is way too old. Drop it.
        if cur_ts < self.start_timestamp:
            return 

        # The packet is in the currrent window.
        if cur_ts <= self.end_timestamp:
            self.current_packets.append(rtp_packet)
        # The packet is in the next window.
        else:
            self.next_packets.append(rtp_packet)

    def flush_packets(self):
            # Process current PCM frames.
            self.emit_pcm_frames(self.process_packets(self.current_packets))
            # Advance us to the next window.
            self.start_timestamp = self.end_timestamp
            self.end_timestamp = self.start_timestamp + self.window_delay
            # we could probably just make this one loop. 
            self.current_packets = [c for c in self.next_packets if c.timestamp <= self.end_timestamp]
            self.next_packets = [c for c in self.next_packets if c.timestamp > self.end_timestamp]
            call_in_the_future(self.window_delay, self.flush_packets)

    def feed_decoder_packet_loss(self, pcm_frames, dropped_packets):
        while dropped_packets > 0:
            pcm_frames.append(self.decoder.decode(None))
            dropped_packets -= 1

    def process_chunks(self, chunks):
        # No audio received in this window. Emit empty frames.
        pcm_frames = []
        if not chunks:
            self.feed_decoder_packet_loss(pcm_frames, self.window_delay / FRAME_SIZE)
            return pcm_frames

        chunks.sort(key=attrgetter('timestamp'))
        # Emit silence between start of the window and the first audio packet.
        last_packet_timestamp = self.start_timestamp
        for packet in chunks:
            # Calculate the gap
            self.feed_decoder_packet_loss(pcm_frames, (packet.timestamp - last_packet_timestamp) / FRAME_SIZE)
            pcm_frames.append(self.decoder.decode(packet.data))
            last_packet_timestamp = packet.timestamp

        self.feed_decoder_packet_loss(pcm_frames, (self.end_timestamp - last_packet_timestamp) / FRAME_SIZE)
        return pcm_frames
```

I have no idea if this will actually work. A more "correct" approach may be to use the sequence number to calculate packet loss, and then feed silence into the decoder as packet loss as well. But I think that using the timestamp may be good enough (this might not be true in practice tho - you should test this). We can let opus do the silence for us, by tricking it into thinking there was packet loss when we're missing data. Which should be a lot more fool-proof than hopefully doing it ourselves.

The above code assumes that the packet's timestamp is in seconds. The packet's raw timestamp is definitely not in seconds though, and may need to be calculated based off of the sampling interval. We could probably kill a bunch of redundant calculation if we operated off of sampling interval instead of seconds for timestamp though. The delta between timestamps would be the number of samples missed - which we could feed to the decoder as packet loss. 

Anyways, the above code is more of a proof of concept of how I think the decoder should work, rather than a correct (in terms of runnable) implementation. There are probably some math errors in dealing with timestamps that need to be corrected. But the main concepts are there:
   
    - rolling fixed-duration window for packets.
    - dropping too old packets
    - ordering of packets for the decoder
    - using simulated packet loss to generate silence.

```py
class ASerializer(Serializer):
    b = MethodField()

        return BSerializer(obj.b, many=True).data

class BSerializer(Serializer):
    a = ASerializer(many=True)

The current implementation also has some problems with inheritance, for example this test will fail:

```py
def test_add_field_inheritance(self):
    class ASerializer(Serializer):
        a = Field()

    class BSerializer(ASerializer):
        pass

    ASerializer.add_field('b', Field())

    a = Obj(a=5, b=2)
    self.assertEqual(BSerializer(a).data['a'], 5)
    self.assertEqual(BSerializer(a).data['b'], 2)
```

I think I would expect adding a field to the base class to also make it available to the child class. A correct implementation of this would have to iterate through all the child classes to add the field. I'm not sure if the extra complexity of this method is worth it, seeing that this is probably not a very common case, and there is a workaround for it.

This is mostly just multiprocessing semantics. The Process object is instantiated in the calling process, so anything you do in __init__ is actually *shared* and will need to be pickled. It may be that 'run' is executed in the subprocess context and is not shared. This would explain why one fails and the other works. Nonetheless, I'd recommend not subclassing multiprocessing.Process. Just define a function and pass it with `target=`.  See https://docs.python.org/3/library/multiprocessing.html

I'm not sure I understand precisely the configuration you are going for, but generally I recommend using multiprocessing when you need to run many consumers in parallel from the same "process".

It is known that kafka-python is currently CPU-bound at scale (which is why I suggest adding CPUs via multiprocessing), but the metrics system is not a component that I have found to be a large contributor. My benchmarks suggest that the network pipe remains the limiting reagent, including serialization / deserialization and socket handling. But this may change as you add more and more topics. I generally benchmark for performance with consumers that are assigned smallish numbers of topic-partitions (~10).

Thinking about this a little more, being able to match based on ID or `==` would also be useful in `DeepSearch` for matching the thing you're searching for. Additionally, I can imagine users wanting to add more complex matching rules, so maybe this is a good opportunity for a hole-in-the-middle approach, something like this:

```
from deepdiff import DeepSearch, Match
from deepdiff.matchers import by_id

DeepSearch(thing1, thing2, matcher=by_id, exclude=[123, 'foo', (lambda x: return x%2 == 0)])
```

This way, users could specify their own matchers, and existing flags like `case_sensitive` could become matcher functions (though it might be good to leave in the kwarg as a shortcut).

Some random thoughts—broad strokes, as promised:

* There are some places that use tuples where a list would be more appropriate (like in `__all__`). The usual is to use lists for homogeneous sequences and tuples for anonymous structs.
* formatters.py looks pretty good (unsurprising, coming from me, since it looks to be mostly my old code).
* Injecting new consts into `curses` is freaky. Why do that?
* There are some DRY issues, like the repeated Python 2/3 compatibility stuff: `if ...: text_type = str`.
* Your Keystroke class looks nice: good API, at first glance. I like that it's a unicode for naive users and simple cases.
* I haven't dug into what `get_keyboard_codes()` and `get_keyboard_sequences()` are used for, but are do they always return the same thing, give the old Python bug that doesn't let us have more than one terminal type per process? If so, maybe we could (magically, lazily) just populate a module from which people can import constants.
* Your attention to the gory details of terminal behavior are amazing and inspirational. You are easily the most expert terminal basher I know. (I'm just reading your "In a perfect world..." comment and marveling at your research and workarounds. Just wow.)
* SequenceTextWrapper: nice! Supporting regex machinery: wow. I'm pretty sure that's a good wow. I wonder if all that stuff takes a long time to initialize, and I consequently wonder if it happens only on demand. SequenceTextWrapper should probably mention something about supporting sequences in its docstring if it's public, rather than just taking TextWrap's.
* I really like your name "Sequence". I guess it could be confused with Python's "sequence" protocol, but my first impression was to like it because it's short, memorable, and uses existing tty vocab.

More to come! (Note to self: resume at terminal.py.)

What `dataSources()` returns depends on the driver, and where the driver looks for odbc.ini files.  Those odbc.ini files could be in a number of places, here's a few suggestions:

- /etc/odbc.ini
- ~/.odbc.ini
- /opt/Progress/DataDirect/Connect_for_ODBC_71/odbc.ini
- ~/Progress/DataDirect/ODBC71/odbc.ini

`$ODBCINI` should point to one of those files, but won't necessarily be the one the driver is looking at.  I would suggest you compare what's in those odbc.ini files with the output from `dataSources()` and see which odbc.ini file pyodbc is looking at (probably).  Then place your DSN info in that file.

I implemented the following workaround which confirms it was a timeout issue with the connection object. Before reusing the connection object I'm testing and reopen a connection if the test fails.

```
 import pyodbc
 try:
 #test current connection object in global variable
      oConnexion = oGlobalVariable['oConnexion']
      oCursor = oConnexion.cursor()
      oCursor.execute("select '1'")
      oCursor.close()
 except:
      #if test fails re-open connection
      oConnexionString = fGetParameterFile(pFileName="global_parameter.ini", pSectionName="AUDIT", pParameterName="oConnexionString")
      oConnexion = pyodbc.connect(oConnexionString, autocommit=True)
      oConnexion.setdecoding(pyodbc.SQL_CHAR, encoding='utf-8')
      oConnexion.setdecoding(pyodbc.SQL_WCHAR, encoding='utf-8')
      oConnexion.setdecoding(pyodbc.SQL_WMETADATA, encoding='utf-8')
      oConnexion.setencoding(encoding='utf-8')
      oGlobalVariable['oConnexion'] = oConnexion
      return oConnexion
 else:
      return oGlobalVariable['oConnexion']
```

> So does each ODBC driver do the math to convert the wire protocol bytes into a standard value that represents a SQL_TYPE_TIMESTAMP?

SQL_TYPE_TIMESTAMP is a SQL / server type, not a C type. If you mean the C types SQL_C_TIMESTAMP/SQL_C_TYPE_TIMESTAMP, then yes --- the driver converts from TDS format into the given structure type.

> Also, do all SQL Server types that map to SQL_TYPE_TIMESTAMP have the same precision and rounding behaviour?

No, the obvious counterexample being datetime2(n) and smalldatetime. The precision and scale can vary and are returned in IPD/IRD fields.


See the links below for more information.

https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/relational-databases/native-client-odbc-date-time/data-type-support-for-odbc-date-and-time-improvements

https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/relational-databases/native-client-odbc-date-time/metadata-parameter-and-result

do this on our training data
do this after we've trained the model, and while we've still got the transformed data available
we will filter the data to the min of 10k rows, or 10% of the training dataset (or the dataset size), both configurable settings
we will then convert the analytics dataset to dense
get the feature names
iterate through the feature names
do some common filtering on feature names
  - [ ] ignore nlp_
  - [ ] dates- keep them. useful to know if something happening later in the day matters compared to it happening earlier
  - [ ] categorical columns- for now, ignore the interactions
grab the original values of that column, and save them
get the std of those values
increment every value in that column by the std
get predictions
save the .describe(include='all') of the predictions
decrement every value by two std
get predictions
save the .describe(include='all') of the predictions
save the value of the std
add a std back, to get the column back to it's starting point

each row in that df is a feature name. each column is something like 'std', 'response from increasing by 1 std', 25th percentile response from increasing by 1 std, etc.

Aakash,


> Both devices are on the same network, and the Pi definitely receives 
> the 'WhoIs' request as it issues an 'IAm response'. The only problem 
> is that this 'IAm' response doesn't seem to be properly processed by 
> bacpypes afterwards.
tl;dr - check the 'address' part of the INI file and make sure you 
provide both the IP address and network size in CIDR notation.  Your 
application is not listening on the broadcast address.  It should be 
something like 192.168.0.12/24

There are two different UDP broadcast addresses (actually three, but one 
of them hasn't been used in decades), all F's and all F's in the host 
portion of the address.  Let's say you have 192.168.0.12 on your machine 
with a subnet mask 255.255.255.0, the two most common broadcast 
addresses seen in the wild would be 255.255.255.255 and 192.168.0.255.

The 255.255.255.255 broadcast address is the address of the "zero 
network" or 0.0.0.0 and it reaches all devices, even those that are on 
different subnets when there is more than one subnet on the same 
(usually Ethernet) network.  Assume that the broadcast is a request, 
like Who-Is.  A device with address 192.168.1.16 would receive the 
broadcast directly but it could not reply with a unicast response (the 
I-Am) without going through a router which breaks symmetry.

For BACpypes applications to listen for unicast messages for the IP 
address and broadcast messages on the subnet broadcast address it opens 
up two sockets, and if you run 'netstat' or something similar you can 
see them both.  The UDP layer in BACpypes also uses it to translate the 
generic '*' meaning "local broadcast" into the appropriate broadcast IP 
address.

If you have the 'netifaces' Python module installed you can also use the 
name of the interface like 'eth0' or 'en1' and it will gather the IP 
address and mask from that.

I'm guessing the deafult word breaks include `=` so that you could tab complete something as both `--foo <tab>` and `--foo=<tab>` or similar. The same is likely true for `:`, which is being worked around specifically [here](https://github.com/kislyuk/argcomplete/blob/308255ed67e32ce2d68d624d108aa2590c8b5d36/argcomplete/__init__.py#L511). The "mangling" is the same thing @asmeurer is talking about where bash views the completion as starting after the `=`, and so inserts the entire completion after that point, resulting in `numpy=numpy=1.`.

This should be fixable by doing the following:
1. **Not** treating `COMP_WORDBREAKS` as "special characters" (i.e. characters that need to be escaped) [here](https://github.com/kislyuk/argcomplete/blob/308255ed67e32ce2d68d624d108aa2590c8b5d36/argcomplete/__init__.py#L515)
2. Extending the `:` fix [here](https://github.com/kislyuk/argcomplete/blob/308255ed67e32ce2d68d624d108aa2590c8b5d36/argcomplete/__init__.py#L513) to any characters that are in `COMP_WORDBREAKS` but are not otherwise considered special to the shell (since they will be escaped regardless).

It's also worth considering this would be going above and beyond what bash's default tab completion is capable of. Creating files called `foo=bar` or `foo:bar` and trying to tab complete them exhibits these same problems.

An alternative might be to expose this concept to the completers somehow, but I'm not entirely sure what that would look like.

Thanks for the report. This is intended behaviour: by default, `stdout` is treated as a raw byte stream. The `encoding` argument claims to change the captured output:

> `encoding` -- if set, this is used to decode any output. By default, any output is treated as raw bytes. If set, the raw bytes are decoded before writing to the passed `stdout` and `stderr` arguments (if set) and before setting the output attributes on the result.

However, from a quick look at the source code, I suspect I erred and this is only happening to the output attributes on the result. The code you've suggested doesn't work since it'll fail when the output is the first byte of a multi-byte character.

import requests, itchat, platform, subprocess, os

from PIL import Image
import sys

BLOCK = 'MM'
class QRCode():
    def __init__(self, fileName, size, padding = 0, background = 'BLACK'):
        self.size = size
        self.padding = padding
        self.img = Image.open(fileName)
        self.times = self.img.size[0]/(size + padding * 2)
        self.rgb = self.img.convert('RGB')
        self.white = BLOCK if background == 'BLACK' else '  '
        self.black = '  ' if background == 'BLACK' else BLOCK
    def print_qr(self):
        sys.stdout.write(' '*50 + '\r')
        sys.stdout.flush()
        print(self.white * (self.size + 2))
        startPoint = self.padding + 0.5
        for y in range(self.size):
            sys.stdout.write(self.white)
            for x in range(self.size):
                r,g,b = self.rgb.getpixel(((x + startPoint) * self.times, (y + startPoint) * self.times))
                sys.stdout.write(self.white if r > 127 else self.black)
            print(self.white)
        print(self.white * (self.size + 2))

Hi.

There is a story behind this. I'll summarize the main point:

It was a conscious decision to only display an en-passant square, when there is a legal en-passant move. This even goes a step further than [X-FEN](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/X-FEN), which has en-passant squares only when there is a pseudo-legal en-passant move.

I usually cite this argument, which I picked up from talkchess: "If one thinks that `rnbqkbnr/pppp1ppp/8/4p3/4P3/8/PPPP1PPP/RNBQKBNR w KQkq - 0 2` and `rnbqkbnr/pppp1ppp/8/4p3/4P3/8/PPPP1PPP/RNBQKBNR w KQkq e6 0 2` are different positions, then they are playing a different game."

As an avid supporter of doing it this way, I'd be intrested in any software that is incomptable with it or real-world scenarios, where it is problematic.

If you absolutely need it (untested):

```python
def oldstyle_fen(board):
    builder = []
    builder.append(board.board_fen())
    builder.append("w" if board.turn == chess.WHITE else "b")
    builder.append(board.castling_xfen())
    builder.append(chess.SQUARE_NAMES[board.ep_square] if board.ep_square else "-")
    builder.append(str(board.halfmove_clock))
    builder.append(str(board.fullmove_number))
    return " ".join(builder)
```

At this point reading from compressed files is fully supported with automatic file detection.

The remaining work is to implement writing to these files formats. I think the best way to do this will be:
1. Create an abstraction over file writing so that the `to_` methods stay clean of implementation details of how exactly to write the file (like reading files doesn't need to know either)
2. The `to_` methods will gain an argument like `compression=None` which when set to a compression string (eg `xz` or `gz`) will write the file compressed.

The original issues were supposed to have both writing/reading, but I think they are separate enough tasks to create new issues for them.

So I've done some further research, and it does seem like this is a problem with the windows version of `fact_path`. It *should* put info under `ansible_local`, like all the other OSes do. Ansible-cmdb can then detect those facts and includes them in the "Custom facts" section of each host.

Unfortunately, there's not much I can do on the Ansible-cmdb end to fix this, since there is no way for Ansible-cmdb to determine which facts are supposed to be "Custom facts". Filing a bug with ansible itself is unlikely to result in any changes, as they generally don't break backwards-compatibility for things such as facts.

The only solution I can see is for you to write a small post-fact-gather script that loads all facts and fixes the problem by moving `ansible_facts["ansible_ShowInstalledPackages"]` into `ansible_facts["ansible_local"]["InstalledPackages"]` instead.

> I have many "HID Commands" defined, but I did not want to use plenty of Report Descriptors for that...

I'm an embedded guy myself, the part usually left out by firmware designers is that the HID higher level abstractions provide better abstractions for the applications guys on the host. Example, a nice HID descriptor with custom vendor page usages, with proper documentation, can be leveraged to move port your features to a different microcontroller that might have a different endpoint size, or new features.

So, the minimum effort HID descriptor for a data pipe is just to have a value array, using 0~255 values to cover the full byte range.

At this point you might want to keep a fixed report Id prefix in your descriptor, this gives you flexibility for future features, yep, it consumes one extra byte though.

Another possibility, a little bit awkward but valid, is to skip the report id in the descriptor but still use your custom value array usage. 

I remember fixing the library for the lack of a report Id. So, it shall work.

If you keep the value array usage, you can upgrade your pipe protocol using a different usage identifier, in this way, you can handle two devices SKUs with the same firmware, or use this when upgrading, etc.

Indeed the issue was numeric fields, but the issue seems to be twofold. 

On the one hand, pyshp usually reads numeric fields, but in this case it failed because the driver that wrote the shapefile wrote int fields "incorrectly" as floats, that is with the ".0" at the end. The numeric fields that fail are all defined as ints (decimal=0), so pyshp tries to convert the string to int, but calling int on a float string doesn't work. 

On the other hand, this was caused by an attempt in v1.2.11 to root out errors in the reading of field types and make it more robust. In doing so it was made a lot stricter in the expected values, assuming perfectly written shapefiles. 

This seems to be the inverse problem of #99, where users had trouble writing ints or strings to float fields etc.

I think the best solution now is to make it backwards compatible with the more lenient approach of v1.2.10, and handle minor data type errors in shapefiles by adding forced conversion. This has now been fixed in the 1.2.x branch and the master branch on GitHub, and will be included in release 1.2.12 shortly and the upcoming major version 2.0.0.

I was able to have a more detailed read of your code. I think your push to add feature and featurecollection geo interfaces is really great and much needed, but on second thought duplication isnt really an issue here. Consider the following approach: 

1. Keep the main geojson implemention with the _Shape object, as it currently stands. 
2. For _ShapeRecord, the geo interface method creates a dictionary of type "Feature" and for the "geometry" entry simply populate it by calling on the `.shape.__geo_interface__`, so no duplication needed. For the "properties" entry populate it using `.fields` and `.record`, which you rightly added. 
3. I don't think we should add a separate _ShapeRecords (plural) object, more natural to stick with the native list type. Besides, I think it would be much more natural to have the feature collection as the geo interface on the Reader object. No duplication needed here either, simply creating the list of features by calling geo interface for each _ShapeRecord. The featurecollection bbox can be read directly from the reader. 

With the mixin class, you are essentially outsourcing the geo interface behavior with a bunch of conditions on the type, instead of just keeping the code logically grouped to each class. 

If this makes sense, create a new PR in line with this modified approach, and I will be happy to merge it.

This example uses the Vanilla Python async library (https://github.com/cablehead/vanilla.consul)
to create a ttl session with a background renew loop to keep the session alive, and then uses that session to create ephemeral kv entries. When the process dies, it's kv entries will be deleted.


```python
import vanilla

import logging
logging.basicConfig()


def init_session(h):
    c = h.consul()

    session_id = c.session.create(behavior='delete', ttl=10).recv()

    @h.spawn
    def _():
        while True:
            print c.session.renew(session_id).recv()
            h.sleep(5000)

    return session_id


h = vanilla.Hub()

session_id = init_session(h)

c = h.consul()

print c.kv.put('%s/foo' % session_id, 'bar', acquire=session_id).recv()
```

def locked_action(key, f):
  lockKey = key + '/.lock'

  c = consul.Consul()

  session = None
  try:
    session = c.session.create(name="consul-scripting-helper locked_action " + key)

    # Acquire lock
    acquired = False
    index = None
    while not acquired:
      # Look for an existing lock, blocking until not taken
      index, data = c.kv.get(lockKey, consistency='consistent', index=index)
      logging.debug("'get' returned: {0}".format((index, data)))
      if data is not None:
        if data['Flags'] != lockFlagValue:  # same as https://github.com/hashicorp/consul/blob/v0.8.3/api/lock.go#L197
          raise Exception('Existing key does not match lock use (lockKey: {0})'.format(lockKey))
        if 'Session' in data:  # if somebody else has the lock we can't acquire it; same as https://github.com/hashicorp/consul/blob/v0.8.3/api/lock.go#L204
          logging.debug("lock is already held by session '{0}', retrying".format(data['Session']))
          continue

      acquired = c.kv.put(lockKey, socket.gethostname(), acquire=session, flags=lockFlagValue)

    # Now we have the lock.

    # We need to track the index at which we acquired it, so that we can pass
    # that to `delete` below.
    acquired_index, _ = c.kv.get(lockKey, consistency='consistent')

    try:
      # Perform action.
      return f()
    except:
      logging.exception('Exception in locked action')
      raise
    finally:

      # Delete lock key (which will automatically release the lock)

      # We need to call `delete` with `cas=acquired_index`, so that we can
      # only delete if nobody else has acquired it since (otherwise we would
      # be deleting their lock under their feet).
      # We _should_ own the lock, but it may not be so if an operator or health
      # check forcefully took it away from us. In that case don't want
      # the program to continue as an assumption is violated, so we raise.
      did_delete = c.kv.delete(lockKey, cas=acquired_index)
      if did_delete:
        logging.debug("deleted lock")
      else:
        raise Exception('lock deletion failed; perhaps an operator or health check took the lock way from us (lockKey: {0})'.format(lockKey))

      c.session.destroy(session)
  except:
    if session is not None:
      logging.error('Exception while holding consul session, destroying session')
      c.session.destroy(session)
    raise
```

You can instantiate an `io.BytesIO()` from the decompressed output and then call `readline()` on that. But that requires buffering the output, which of course has performance issues for large outputs.

The proper solution is for python-zstandard to provide an API to obtain an object that conforms to the `io.RawIOBase` interface. This is how `gzip.open()` and friends work. There is definite value in providing that API out of the box. I'll add it to the TODO list.

Complexity of input space - somewhat similar to the ES-HyperNEAT question of how to distinguish the best places to add a new neuron (the original dissertation was using weight variance, but stated that something else might well work better). One interesting variant of Novelty Search was "Novelty of thinking" - monitoring the hidden nodes as well as the outputs to see how variable responses were to the inputs; it wound up generalizing better. That was with fixed-topology networks, but the authors remarked that it could be done using NEAT's homology-detection innovation numbers to see what to compare with what.

An extension onto NEAT-Python for mpi may do best, as IIRC @CodeReclaimers stated elsewhere, as a dependent project, so that NEAT-Python can remain using Python only; having a shared interface between the pure-Python and mpi4py variants of the distributed computing code would be very helpful for such, and also allow for other implementations. (Exactly how coupled processes need to be between machines - as in how efficient inter-process communication needs to be - is going to vary depending on the evaluation function, of course.) @bennr01 now has available an ARM-cluster, but it still needs setting up.

This has nothing to do with flask-restplus, besides it has been answered a couple of times already.

Anyway, in most of the cases, when your application runs behind a reverse-proxy (which is probably the case here although I don't have a significant experience with k8s), you need to tell your application so.

Here is the Flask [documentation](http://flask.pocoo.org/docs/0.12/deploying/wsgi-standalone/#proxy-setups).

But in your case, the ProxyFix might not work properly due to the fact your application is hosted behind an "alternative root path" (ie. `/prefix`). Thankfully, there is a documented workaround [here](http://flask.pocoo.org/snippets/35/).
Now, as you may not have access to the reverse-proxy configuration to add such headers, you may want to write your own *Fixer* this way:

```python
MY_PREFIX = '/prefix'

class ReverseProxied(object):
    '''Wrap the application in this middleware and configure the 
    front-end server to add these headers, to let you quietly bind 
    this to a URL other than / and to an HTTP scheme that is 
    different than what is used locally.

    :param app: the WSGI application
    '''
    def __init__(self, app):
        self.app = app

    def __call__(self, environ, start_response):
        script_name = MY_PREFIX
        environ['SCRIPT_NAME'] = script_name
        path_info = environ['PATH_INFO']
        if path_info.startswith(script_name):
            environ['PATH_INFO'] = path_info[len(script_name):]

        scheme = environ.get('HTTP_X_SCHEME', '')
        if scheme:
            environ['wsgi.url_scheme'] = scheme
        return self.app(environ, start_response)

```

And then fix your app this way:

```python
app = Flask(__name__)
app.wsgi_app = ReverseProxied(app.wsgi_app)
```

By caching, I only meant [Spark RDD persistence](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/rdd-programming-guide.html#rdd-persistence), i.e. if your upstream preprocessing of data requires caching/persisting the RDD into Spark storage memory... If not (i.e. your just reading from disk), then you can tune down the [spark.memory.storageFraction](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/configuration.html), so less of your memory is "reserved" for RDD storage.

As for the queue, that was required to guarantee that the TF process (running in the background) could access the data being fed via Spark executor process (in the foreground).  A workaround would be to switch your code to InputMode.TENSORFLOW and use TF's built in `tf.data` APIs to read directly from HDFS.

My comment in #4746 

One of the reason for this issue is that you run the python command in the exact directory you install MXNet.

Say that you clone the MXNet project into /home/user1/incubator-mxnet, you build the mxnet library then go to the python directory to run install command python setup.py install --user.

After that , please exit the python directory before you run python to test mxnet.

As there is a directory named mxnet in the incubator-mxnet/python directory, if you run python in directory incubator-mxnet/python and then import mxnet as mx, python will try to import the directory instead of the mxnet lib.

In conclusion, do not start python shell in the incubator-mxnet/python directory, or else you will get the cannot import name libinfo error message.

Please create a new issue to report it if you didn't run python shell in incubator-mxnet/python while you get the same error message cannot import name libinfo

Definitely not having two versions of the hooks is crucial.

For the case of S3: yes, s3 is not a filesystem. But the "fs"-like interfaces should be implemented as method on the existing S3 hook using the existing methods where possible. i.e. `walk()` would use `list_prefixes()` in someway. Anything else is just going to make long-term maintenance too hard.

Multiple inheritance is entirely possible in python, and having:

```
class S3Hook(FsHook, AwsHook):
```

would be reasonable in this case.

It's a little weird and, in fact, the module level functions are a bit weird to begin with, especially since they just pass through to the conf object. They could all be handled by variable assignment instead of function declaration. It might also be worth marking the module direct access as deprecated:

```python

# Historical convenience functions to access config entries

load_test_config = conf.load_test_config
get = conf.get
getboolean = conf.getboolean
getfloat = conf.getfloat
getint = conf.getint
has_option = conf.has_option
remove_option = conf.remove_option
as_dict = conf.as_dict
set = conf.set

from zope.deprecation import deprecated as _deprecated
for func in [load_test_config, get, getboolean, getfloat, getint, has_option,
             remove_option, as_dict]:
    _deprecated(
        func,
        "Accessing configuration method '{f.__name__}' directly from "
        "the configuration module is deprecated. Please access the "
        "configuration from the 'configuration.conf' object via "
        "'conf.{f.__name__}'".format(f=func))
```

@sajjadsaleem I don't know if there are hard an fast _requirements_. However, there are some things which we have found to work.
- Audio recorded at least at 16Khz 16bit or more and in mono (stereo _may_ work too)
- Audio fed to the system at 16Khz 16bit mono
- Text "normalized" so that the transcription corresponds to exactly what is in the audio
- Audio segmented into segments of about 1-15 seconds before being fed to the system
- Audio need _not_ be perfectly without noise
- Audio should reflect the environment in which the system is to be used (If used in noisy area, the audio should be noisy too so the system learns to deal with noise.)

As for supporting Urdu you'll need to make changes similar to those required for French support which is described here[[1](https://discourse.mozilla.org/t/tutorial-how-i-trained-a-specific-french-model-to-control-my-robot/22830/48)] or German described here[[2](https://tilmankamp.github.io/FOSDEM2018/)].

This way, in Python 2 `jsonpickle.encode('foo')` would yield `'{"py/bytes": "foo"}'` and not just `"foo"` which may be excessively verbose.

The current behaviour in Python 2 is this (which seems quite broken):
```python
>>> jsonpickle.encode('\xff')
'"\xff"'
>>> jsonpickle.decode(jsonpickle.encode('\xff'))
ValueError: Invalid UTF-8 sequence length when decoding 'string'
```

I don't quite understand this problem. Please provide more explanation and I can re-open

scenario 1
- worker 1 decides that Task has resource A
- you decide to change the code so Task has resource B instead
- you deploy the code and start worker 2 to schedule Task with resource B
- while this scheduling is happening, your assistant worker picks up an instance of Task, then has worker 2 change it to use resource B while it's already running, freeing up resource A for the assistant to get another task
- by repeating this, your assistant can end up running arbitrarily many instances of Task, ignoring your resource limitation

scenario 2
- worker 1 assigns resource A to Task, which is batch runnable
- you change the code so it's resource B
- you deploy fresh assistants but do not start scheduling yet
- the assistant is able to pick up batches of tasks with resource A but reports back a task with resource B
- because resource A is never used and nothing in the scheduler is limited by resource B, this can be repeated to create arbitrarily many batches of Task

I do not like caching the outputs on the QGIS side. Consider for instance an output generated by Julio that must be seen by 3-4 other people in the hazard group (+ other people in Colombia). If we cache it server-side we can do the work only once. To start, we could store the outputs in some subdirectory of /tmp (assuming we have enough space there). We could use the following logic:

1. If the calculation was started with `oq engine` do nothing, the export will be done later only if the user calls `oq engine --export-output`, without any cache.
2. If the calculation was started from the webui at the end of it runs an `oq extract hcurves/all`; then the outputs will be available in the cache until the cache is cleared.

I have to figure out how to make the export work for different export types (i.e. XML, CSV) but probably we can produce all of them from an intermediate representation in HDF5 stored in the cache.

Regarding adding a license notice, from the GPLv2 source:

> To do so, attach the following notices to the program.  It is safest
to attach them to the start of each source file to most effectively
convey the exclusion of warranty; and each file should have at least
the "copyright" line and a pointer to where the full notice is found.

Note it says "safest" there. There is ambiguity in how we can choose to apply the license. In this case, we are distributing the project via Github, which has multiple ways of pointing out the license. If a user chooses to copy a file out and disregard the license, they can do so quite easily and strip the license. We're also highly unlikely to go after anyone choosing to do so, and therefore I think we are okay to continue along our path (IANAL and don't take what I'm saying as anything coming from a lawyer).

I've already spent my entire morning thinking about this issue and I believe we are in compliance. The entire project is currently under GPLv2. I believe that information is already prominently displayed. We are currently uninterested in relicensing the project.

Had the same issue on Opensuse Leap (bug : https://bugzilla.opensuse.org/show_bug.cgi?id=999296 ) with Python 3. The problem (at least in my case)  is a specific Python compilation flag which can be missing on some distros (Opensuse Leap is missing it, however Opensuse Tumbleweed, Debian and Centos have it)
You should check your Python compilation flags which seem to be missing the "--enable-loadable-sqlite-extensions", this can be done in the Python shell with a command . I had to recompile Python myself with the flag enabled to make it works on Leap.
The other solution is to use a VM with Centos or Debian, as they both have Python compiled with this flag.

2. Do `pipenv install some-package`. pipenv thinks it is not included in `Pipfile` and add a duplicate line `some-package` into it.

But also it was an obvious coding fault: mutate a dict without ref to the original object. I don't see why this should be rejected.

SemVer and PEP 440 are inherently incompatible, so I don't know that you need to blame it on the command. :smile:

Okay, so I just had this issue so decided to go looking at the code... The reason would seem (from a quick skim) that everything is in the https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/blob/master/pipenv/cli.py file, which has ~58 lines of imports. Of course, 99% of these probably aren't necessary to generate the ~8 fairly static lines for completion!

I'd say the solution would be to split the CLI commands out into at least something like `cli` for parsing, and `cli_heavy` where commands are actually run (and do all the importing there, _after_ the arg parser has done it's thing).

I might get around to trying something like this in a bit. If not, it's documented here from others :)

The problem is somewhere in bdist_wheel. It changes the executable property of the build_scripts command to just 'python' and that is rewritten on install. Unfortunately by explicitly running the build command it must be overwriting the executable property again.
It might be possible to modify bdist_wheel so that build runs twice in your case, with the correct (for wheel) shebang, or to otherwise make sure bdist_wheel controls the sequence of operations more effectively.
A different solution would be to use setuptools' entry_points scripts, which should not have this problem.

https://bitbucket.org/pypa/wheel/src/5d49f8cf18679d1bc6f3e1e414a5df3a1e492644/wheel/bdist_wheel.py?at=default&fileviewer=file-view-default#bdist_wheel.py-189

What should happen if the requirements.txt includes syntax other than simple lists of requirements (perhaps with environment markers)? Presumably comments could be ignored, but what about options to pip like `-i` or `-e`?

Already, the [guidance by the python packaging user's guide](https://packaging.python.org/discussions/install-requires-vs-requirements/#install-requires-vs-requirements-files) describes the distinct purposes intended for requirements.txt versus 'install_requires' and similar. By referencing a requirements.txt file for a package's declared minimum required versions, it violates some of the goals of a requirements.txt (showing pinned versions for an environment).

How do we reconcile these challenges?

Looking at the code for `doctest.testmod()`, we see that the module to be tested is resolved using `sys.modules['__main__']`. Because the entry point was used to launch the script, `__main__` is the setuptools script and not mymodule. I don't think there's anything that setuptools can or should do about this condition, though if you can, please suggest it.

One possible workaround is to invoke your script with `-m`:

```
python -m  mymodule --test
```

Or alternately, invoke testmod with an explicit reference to the current module:

```
def main():
    mod = sys.modules[__name__]
    doctest.testmod(mod)
```

Because having duplicate entries in a PATH is annoying and wasteful and
renders the activate "script" non-idempotent. If the virtual environment is
already in the PATH for some other reason, activate will currently make it
a duplicate entry.  However, this is based on the premise that virtualenv
behaves in a sane manner, but frankly the practice of sourcing the main
script to begin the environment is so fundamentally flawed that there's
really no chance of saving this software.  I've closed the pull request
because I really don't think there's much point.  Rather than sourcing
activate to begin and setting up a fragile 'deactivate' function in the
host shell, it would be *much* cleaner to start a new shell and then exit
it to exit the virtual environment.  AFAICT, the only reason to source the
activate script instead of starting a new shell is to preserve the prompt,
and that's not really a good trade off for the fragility.

Hi @jmwri, thanks for being in touch. Since the Kafka API currently doesn't support creating topics via the binary protocol, the only way that pykafka could support it is by wrapping the CLI tools with python calls. We've considered this and have decided that it's not a high priority because of the simplicity with which python allows one to shell out (`subprocess.popen("kafka-topics.sh --create --zookeeper localhost:2181 --topic testtopic2 --replication-factor 1 --partitions 1".split())`). If we wrapped the CLI, we'd effectively be doing just that. That said, if you feel it's useful enough, I'd support a pull request that added topic creation and deletion via the CLI to the `pykafka/cli` module.

Thank you @emmett9001 for reviewing. I agree it'd be good to leverage an existing kwarg, but wouldn't that shadow the existing functionality of `reset_offset_on_start`? My impression is that currently the param only determines if offset reset is to be done immediately upon consumer start, without impacting any subsequent offset resets. However, for the read-only consume use case, we would like to prevent any offset reset attempts throughout the lifetime of a consumer.
Doing it in this PR makes sense. Once that's in, I'll give this a detailed review and leave comments, if any.

(For later reference: [as per their docs][0], kazoo considers `ConnectionLoss` et al to be part of their private API, so we might have to check what exactly we should catch - and also, which of their errors are considered intermittent and which are permanent, so we don't swallow the latter too)

[0]: http://kazoo.readthedocs.org/en/2.2.1/api/exceptions.html

The nasty thing is, it would still potentially reorder messages even if you set `max_retries=0` (which I would expect to guarantee ordering). In practice, I only ever hit a queue-full when really hammering the producer (in benchmarking), so it might be reasonable to leave it at raising a NotImplementedError from the constructor, for now.

I found out that we indeed have the stats, and 99% of our users are using `requests` -- my apologies. Given so, we do want to roll-out our own retry logic for consistency with our libs in other languages. One simplification is that we can treat error from non-request libs as non-retryable; we maintain the behavior of non-request client. But we still want to put retry logic outside the underlying http-client, and trigger retry logic for `requests` error. Let me clear up any possible misunderstandings: 

- Invenio `maint-1.x` (and `maint-2.x`) branches are being supported regarding integration of bug fixes. There are many users using Invenio 1.x code base still.

- Invenio `legacy` branch contains about 30 commits or so over the `maint-1.2` branch. It can be used for new legacy feature pull requests -- especially if the said feature has been actively deployed and used on some service instance for a long time. E.g. CDS uses the `legacy` branch in this way. 

- Invenio `legacy` branch may eventually constitute a new Invenio 1.3 release, if there will be interest.  This would be the last legacy code base feature release, as was discussed [during IUGW2015](https://indico.cern.ch/event/441770/contributions/1093934/attachments/1168404/1685482/iugw2015-invenio-state-of-the-union.pdf). 

- @jirikuncar is triaging many old "stalled" issues. If there is an easy `maint-1.x` or `legacy` PR, then please submit and reopen. If there is no easy legacy PR, or if it is not worth creating a new service-agnostic legacy PR, then it's better to "sleep" the feature regarding the `legacy` branch, and think of implementing it in the new Invenio 3 code base only.

The [Python bug](http://bugs.python.org/issue15657) that @tseaver cited is still open in 3.6 and master, so some sort of fix is still needed. However, it's been open since2012 and, although there was a patch to change the value of `METH_KEYWORDS`, what actually got *merged* was a change to the documentation.

CPython itself never uses `METH_KEYWORDS` by itself, as far as I can see. It's always combined with `METH_VARARGS`. zope.interface, on the other hand, was using `METH_KEYWORDS` by itself; the documentation that was removed implied that this was acceptable, but the implementation didn't allow this. The proposed fix in CPython changed `METH_KEYWORDS` to be `0x2 | METH_VARARGS`, which is the patch that was applied to zope.interface. 

However, that results in the above warning.

So perhaps the better fix for zope.interface would be to change uses of the bare `METH_KEYWORDS` to `METH_KEYWORDS | METH_VARARGS` and remove the redefinition. As far as I can see, this is correct for Python 2.7 and onward.

Hi @igsm! Thanks for your feedback!

I understand that receiving a token just after activation (or just after registration in some cases) is very handy for ALI clients (frontend web apps or mobile apps). However there are two reasons we do not do that:

1) As you noticed we need to support a few API auth methods so it is very difficult do it in a very generic way. Especially there are DRF auth backends that are not based on any type of token, like basic auth or session. Also JWT differs from DRF `auth_token` method.

2) We believe that one endpoint should do one thing correctly and in a secure way. That's why we love to think about endpoints as they are orthogonal actions.

Now, knowing the above if you really need authenticating users in your flow after activation then we suggest to override `ActivationView` and generate JWT token to response on successful activation in a manual way.

@piotr-szpetkowski On the other hand I believe we can do the above customization easier in the future by utilizing custom serializers. `ActivationView` does not care about `self.serializer.data` but if it would care about that then the customization would be straightforward.

```python
# alternative approach that works when the thread got an exception as well
import threading
try:
    import jnius
except:
    jnius = None

if jnius:
    orig_thread_run = threading.Thread.run
    def thread_check_run(*args, **kwargs):
        try:
            return ret
        finally:
            jnius.detach()
    threading.Thread.run = thread_check_run
```

>>I'm not sure I understand. If ChromaticOpticalPSF.dilate() were left undefined (to take one example), 
then the parent class method ChromaticObject.dilate() would take over and wrap the 
ChromaticOpticalPSF inside a ChromaticObject.

>Maybe I'm doing something wrong in how I've set up this subclass, but that's not how it worked when I tried it.

Hmm...  I guess it's obvious now that I didn't actually try this, sorry about that!  I should have said that I would have expected the `ChromaticOpticalPSF` object to get wrapped in a `ChromaticObject`.

Looking more carefully at your code, it appears the reason the wrapper didn't work is that you've called 
`super(ChromaticOpticalPSF, self).__init__(self)` inside of `ChromaticOpticalPSF.__init__()`, which seems like a perfectly reasonable thing to do.  The problem is that this then defines the `._A` and `._fluxFactor` attributes which then confuses `._applyMatrix()` into *not* changing the type of `ChromaticOpticalPSF` when you call, for example, `.dilate()`.  I think this boils down to the fact that the class `ChromaticObject` is attempting to do two things: A) it's the base class for all the other chromatic classes, B) it's the object that handles transformations.  This leads to some weirdness, for instance, a directly instantiated `ChromaticObject` always has a `.obj` attribute, but some of its subclasses won't, like `ChromaticSum`.

Personally, I think this behavior is probably okay, and we can work around it (in particular I think this might be simpler than, say, creating a `ChromaticTransform` subclass of `ChromaticObject`.)  One possibility would be to make a `ChromaticObject.wrap(obj)` method that takes care of defining `._A` and `._fluxFactor`, or maybe just making `obj` an optional instead of required keyword to the `ChromaticObject.__init__` method so that `._A` and `._fluxFactor` only get defined if one is explicitly trying to wrap an object.

Sure:
* In social software Mastodon, [an icon is needed](https://github.com/tootsuite/mastodon/issues/1249) for a button to add a warning to a content.
* In any app, you could need an icon for an action starting by « Flag as… ». The rectangle flag could work, but the triangle one seems to work better as a signifier of being marked for what it is. The triangle put the emphasis on the item it’s applied on, while the rectangle seem to be a symbol for itself, possibly meaning Language or Country or Internationalisation.
* As an icon added on a map, a triangle flag would mean something different than a rectangle one. “I’ve been there”, “Here is burried the treasure”, anything that wants to mark the place and not the flag as important.
* A “Report” feature on social or comment software may benefit to this kind of flag too.
* A golf app would need a triangle flat. A minesweeper game clone would need it too. (Those are not serious examples)

> Patroni MUST have write access to `postgresql.conf` and `pg_hba.conf` and as far as I can recall, entire `/etc/postgresql` is owned by root. It would be great to mention this fact in documentation.

On debian, it's owned by `postgres` so if you run Patroni under the `postgres` user, which I guess is the way we should deploy it, it's ok.
```
# ls -l /etc/postgresql/9.4/main/
total 48
-rw-r--r-- 1 postgres postgres     0 Jul  1 11:37 custom.conf
-rw-r--r-- 1 postgres postgres   315 Jul  1 21:21 environment
-rw-r--r-- 1 postgres postgres   143 Jul  1 21:21 pg_ctl.conf
-rw-r----- 1 postgres postgres  4641 Jul  1 21:21 pg_hba.conf
-rw-r----- 1 postgres postgres  1636 Jul  1 21:21 pg_ident.conf
-rw-r--r-- 1 postgres postgres 21026 Jul  1 21:21 postgresql.conf
-rw-r--r-- 1 postgres postgres   378 Jul  1 21:21 start.conf
```
But, yes, it would be great to mention this fact in the documentation in case of custom deployment.

> There are two methods, `save_configuration_files` and `restore_configuration_files`: https://github.com/zalando/patroni/blob/master/patroni/postgresql.py#L1361
These methods should still make/restore a backups of configuration files to/from data directory.
Why it's important? Because we really want these configuration files to be a backed up together with entire data.
Why do we have these two methods? Because for example `wal-e` for some reason is skipping all `*.conf` file while backing up cluster.

The `*.backup` files are generated by Patroni, we could let them in the data directory. `pg_basebackup` is backuping them:
```
$ pg_lsclusters 
Ver Cluster Port Status        Owner    Data directory                 Log file
9.4 node01  5433 online        postgres /var/lib/postgresql/9.4/node01 /var/log/postgresql/postgresql-9.4-node01.log
9.4 node02  5434 down,recovery postgres /var/lib/postgresql/9.4/node02 /var/log/postgresql/postgresql-9.4-node02.log
$ ls -l /var/lib/postgresql/9.4/node01/{pg_hba,postgresql}.conf.backup
-rw-r----- 1 postgres postgres 412 Jul  1 18:52 /var/lib/postgresql/9.4/node01/pg_hba.conf.backup
-rw-r--r-- 1 postgres postgres 474 Jul  1 18:52 /var/lib/postgresql/9.4/node01/postgresql.conf.backup
$ rm -rf /var/lib/postgresql/9.4/node02
$ pg_basebackup -p 5433 -U repl -R -D /var/lib/postgresql/9.4/node02
Password: 
NOTICE:  pg_stop_backup complete, all required WAL segments have been archived
$ ls -l /var/lib/postgresql/9.4/node02/{pg_hba,postgresql}.conf.backup
-rw-r----- 1 postgres postgres 412 Jul  1 21:17 /var/lib/postgresql/9.4/node02/pg_hba.conf.backup
-rw-r--r-- 1 postgres postgres 474 Jul  1 21:17 /var/lib/postgresql/9.4/node02/postgresql.conf.backup
```

Je reproduis le problème, mais je ne suis pas sûr de comprendre d'où ça vient:
Les parsers sont [bien déclarés](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-web-api/blob/master/setup.py#L57) comme une dépendance de web-api (via une [dépendance optionnelle](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-core/blob/master/setup.py#L32) de core). Pourtant, ils ne s'installent pas. 

La solution la plus "simple" que je vois est de continuer à gérer dans mes-aides la cohérence entre la version de France et la version de la Web API. 

Le fix que tu proposes revient à faire de même en gérant la cohérence entre les parsers et france. Ça me paraît un peu moins bien dans la mesure où il est plus naturel pour mes-aides de demander l'installation d'une version spécifique d'une API, plutôt que de demander l'installation spécifique d'un package technique avec lequel il n'interagit pas.

Une fois de plus, le comportement de `pip` pose problème. Comme le dit @MattiSG, l'API preview est aujourd'hui incluse dans Core pour éviter la complexité de ces gestions de dépendances. Peut-être devrait-on considérer d'intégrer aussi à core les parsers et l'API legacy ?

I definitely like the idea of being able to control the argument order, including using groups.  This proposal is better than random.  

However, is it not also still arbitrary that the nested schemas come before the top level schema?  I might expect, on average, that the top level arguments are actually used the most because they specify input files and required inputs, while the nested schemas are generally going to be more subtle details where the defaults are often suitable and only expert users will change them.  Not always, of course.

I wonder if a fully general solution might rather be to mark the schema entries with a decorator.  I believe the decorator functions will be called in declaration order, which would allow us to default to the order the programmer used.  This is very likely to be the best help and processing order most of the time, especially if the programmer is aware that the definition order is to be used this way.

I have pondered the decorator pattern here before, but have been hesitant because it breaks the Marshmallow pattern.

I don't mind accepting the PR this way, because it is definitely better than before.

This use case wasn't fully supported before, but it was an easy fix.  I added some more descriptive error messages and also updated the documentation [here](https://github.com/wq/django-rest-pandas#customizing-renderers).  The error is that if you have a `rest_pandas` renderer in `DEFAULT_RENDERER_CLASSES`, you need to import `rest_pandas` before importing anything from `rest_framework.views` or you may end up with a recursive import.

One way to fix the import order is to add `rest_pandas` to your `INSTALLED_APPS`, though you'll also want to import `PandasMixin` to add to your own views.

If we want to both support a "tree" of states, and arbitrary state addition (which I think is a good idea), I think my preference would be to have a "0" state, which starts out fully analyzed and is only used for tree-traversal purposes, from which our initial state, as well as anything added with just `m.add(state)`, descends from, and then have `add` take an optional kwarg for parent when that isn't desired.

For instance, if we load some binary, our initial state tree is:

```
[0 state] -> [makeLinux(binary)]
```

and running `m.add(s)` gets us:

```
[0 state] -> [makeLinux(binary)
          -> [s]
```

but we can also call `m.add(s', parent=s)` to get:

```
[0 state] -> [makeLinux(binary)
          -> [s] -> [s']
```

The issue with CyLP and CVXOPT is that both packages have Python extensions that link to liblapack and libblas which depend on libgfortran.  The symbols provided by the conda libgfortran libraries are not sufficient for the lapack and blas libraries installed on the system.  Force removing this package results in the system libgfortran library being used.  

A more preferable solution to this would be to not use the system provided liblapack or libblas at all and instead meet these dependencies with conda packages which are linked to the conda provided libgfortran.  This will take time, I will look into it when I move the cylp and cxvopt_glpk recipes over to conda-forge.

@be5invis `pyftsubset` handles TTF components correctly (it will employ a "greedy" tactic, i.e. if there are composite glyphs that you specify in your target subset, it will also include the necessary outline glyphs that are references by the composites. 

As far as `pyftmerge` goes, I have not tested it much, so you’ll need to experiment yourself. 

Finally, in addition to `pyftmerge`, FontLab apps (FontLab Studio 5 and TransType 4) offer font merging. FontLab Studio 5 will let you control what to do with glyphs that have the same names in different fonts (omit, overwrite or append suffix). But it won’t do much smart stuff with other SFNT tables. 

Generally, no magical font merging tool exists that would be supersmart. So the merging part is the toughest. But once you have a big merged font, the `pyftsubset`+`ttx`+`otf2otc` workflow that I described actually does work well. I've tested it. In fact, my tool https://github.com/twardoch/fonttools-utils/tree/master/pyftfeatfreeze can be used for such partial purpose. It remaps the `cmap` table by hardcoding various `GSUB` features, and allows simple renaming in the `name` table. You can generate a number of such fonts with it which will differ only in the `cmap` and `name` tables, and then merge the result using `otf2otc`, and it will work quite nicely. 

But yeah, merging is tedious, and you’ll need to figure out things yourself.

Here's my input, for what it's worth.

I agree that it is important to provide a simple interface that doesn't require the user to deal with page tokens. If we don't, people will often choose to fetch just the first page. I know I've done that.

Whether it is better to return a list or an iterator will depend on the API, as will whether it makes sense to offer a `limit` parameter.

Default limits are rarely going to be appropriate. A default limit on PubSub topics would surely be bad, for instance. Failing to override such a default with `limit=None` would almost always be an insidious bug.

If listing PubSub topics returns an iterator, I actually don't think it makes sense to offer a `limit` parameter at all. I can't think of a use-case for it. If the user really did want to retrieve some limited number of arbitrary topics, they could do that by terminating the iteration early.

@eoogbe: it's also not safe to assume that the only problems that can ever happen during an RPC happen during RPC invocation. It's perfectly possible, for example, for a response-streaming RPC to stream one hundred responses and then time out so that the client sees an exception raised from the one-hundred-and-first call to `next(my_response_iterator)`. Or consider another RPC that has streamed fifty responses (and those responses have arrived at the client and been emitted from the response-iterator object) before the user's wireless router falls off the shelf and into the bathtub?

As I assess it `_catch_errors` is not adequate for what you're trying to do. Consider splitting it into `_catch_errors_on_invocation` and `_catch_errors_during_iteration`? Or maybe author some other utility function that given an iterator returns an exception-translating-wrapping of the given iterator? While it may not be the case that `_catch_errors` knows whether or not the RPC invocation is response-unary or response-streaming, something at some level of abstraction must, and I suppose that will be a place where at least part of your fix will have to be made.
