@eddavisson, this looks like a problem of conflicting constraints in the v1beta3 client.  When specifying a localhost, [scheme is not allowed](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-datastore/blob/master/java/datastore/src/main/java/com/google/datastore/v1beta3/client/DatastoreOptions.java#L112).  As a result, `gcloud-java` strips scheme from user input.  However, the check in `DatastoreFactory`'s `validateUrl` fails when the input starts with "127.0.0.1" (an "http://" is necessary for IP addresses).  As a result, input using IP addresses rather than hostnames will not work.  `gcloud-java` uses "localhost" instead of "127.0.0.1", so we didn't encounter this problem in our tests.  A fix will have to come in the client for IP addresses to work.  Depending on which constraint is relaxed in the client, `gcloud-java` may also have to push a fix.

@tom-haines Thanks for pointing this out and providing repro code!  I would like to make a suggestion if you are using code similar to your repro code to set up your own tests.  `gcloud-java`'s [`LocalDatastoreHelper`](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-java/blob/master/gcloud-java-datastore/src/main/java/com/google/cloud/datastore/testing/LocalDatastoreHelper.java) class makes it simpler to set up the tests against the local emulator.  (Note: this helper was renamed and refactored right after 0.1.7; we recommend you upgrade to the latest gcloud-java version 0.2.1.  If you'd like to stay with 0.1.7, the relevant class is [`LocalGcdHelper`](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-java/blob/v0.1.7/gcloud-java-datastore/src/main/java/com/google/gcloud/datastore/testing/LocalGcdHelper.java)).  `gcloud-java`'s test helper will eliminate the need to download and start the gcd script yourself, and it also has an `options()` method that returns options so that you don't have to construct them yourself. (see the [package-info](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-java/blob/master/gcloud-java-datastore/src/main/java/com/google/cloud/datastore/testing/package-info.java) for an example).

The gist is we started with a single stack defining all environments, eventually moved away from it and have a single template that brings up one stack and can be repurposed for multiple environments. Test setup:
1. Gunicorn: worker=gevent, workers=4, and max_requests=5
1. Ran `while [ 1 ]; do curl -s http://127.0.0.1:5020/metrics/ ; done;`  for about 30 seconds
   1. That endpoint returns `generate_latest(registry)`
   1. In `post_request` I update a counter and gauge metric
   1. This setup causes workers to have a short lifetime and stresses the creating/reporting metrics
1. When I stopped the curl command, gauge livesum files would be left for pids that are no longer running.

The race condition is caused by `MultiProcessCollector.collect` getting a list of files and then iterating over it to tally all of the values.  But the worker for that file might have exited between getting the list of filenames and getting the values out of the file.  This causes a new file to be created after the "real" one has been deleted from the woker's `child_exit`. `MultiProcessCollector` should never create files if they don't exist.

The counter files are worrisome.  On active servers these could pile up.  Also I prefer not to create too many local files when I'm running inside containers.  This could easily get into 100s or 1000s of files.  Could we do something like this:
1. On worker exit the counter file gets renamed from `counter_123.db` to `counter_123.db.dead`
1. The first step of `MultiProcessCollector` is to take all `*.dead` files and summarize them into a `summed.db` file.  Then it deletes the `*.dead` file.  (This has race conditions all over it)
1. The remaining `MultiProcessCollector` code sums up the individual live counter files and the single summed file.

Need to think about this more, just thinking out loud here.

> Hmm, how does that work behind the scenes?

From [the source](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Modules/_multiprocessing/semaphore.c), it looks like it's using [WaitForSingleObjectEx](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/desktop/api/synchapi/nf-synchapi-waitforsingleobjectex) syscall in windows, and a [POSIX semaphore](http://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man7/sem_overview.7.html) on Unix.

> I think this is something that should Just Work in any environment, I rather not have a system that requires extensive documentation and reasoning to work - as users will get it wrong.

Fair enough, it's just a lot more work to get there :).

If multiprocessing.Lock is suitable, then I think mark_process_dead could maybe be expanded to aggregate into a single file on worker death by default, using that lock?

Rough stab at general solution: register an atexit function that calls mark_process_dead. That should work for any multiprocess scenario, with the caveat that processes forcibly killed will not run the atexit handle, and thus leak mmapped files. But that's a common enough issue with killing processes that way, and the leak would be limited to rare error scenarios, rather than every process recycle.

If you do that, the client will raise the exception `googlemaps.exceptions._OverQueryLimit` when it hits the limit instead of retrying over and over.

Since address numbers can contain letters and still be valid, it's better practice for the parser to parse '1234B' as `AddressNumber` instead of dividing it into a number and a suffix. That way you'll avoid messing up valid addresses. I'd recommend that you preprocess the address number to split at suffix letters if you know they'll be in there. I added a space between the address number and the suffix in the training data you provided and the parser seemed to work fine.

Okay, let's say we want to rebuild and deploy translations in a rapid way. For instance that a simple spelling error can be fixed quickly on CrowdIn and then deployed.

I observe the following:

1. That ReadTheDocs needs the translations in a Git repository because it will/should not be installing a CrowdIn client and fetching translations on-the-fly.

1. That we need to have *source* documentation hosted in the main repository to keep it tightly aligned with our release process in the process before freezing it and uploading it to CrowdIn.

1. That the size of the main repository matters, and having -- say -- 100x updates from CrowdIn will all too likely have an impact (not saying `100 * 3.3 MB` but still). Especially if we start storing localized screenshots. We now have 95 releases, so it's not unlikely that we'll be doing a lot of pulls from CrowdIn for both documentation and interface translations.

I can think of two different immediate solutions:

a. That we create a separate repository to which we maintain the documentation translations. This repository is the one that's deployed to all the ReadTheDocs project translations, but the main `kolibri.readthedocs.io` remains the main development repo.

b. That we upload the latest versions of translation resources to our own intermediate HTTP service which is then pulled in dynamically during `conf.py` execution (I don't even know if this is possible or if it's too late, haven't experimented)

I'm personally in favor of solving the problem now, to avoid any further damage to the repo's size. And I think we should go for a) because it can be done quite quickly.

As an aside, I believe this issue is analogous to the difficulties we've had saving the most recent progress state when someone navigates away from content.

The way many web apps handle this is to set an 'unsaved-changes' flag somewhere, and then if the user navigates away while that's true you pop up an alert that says "You have unsaved changes - wait?" This pattern works when it's rare (due to fast or infrequent saves) but would get frustrating if it occurred frequently.

Implementing TimeGate should not take long as some of the binary lookup and redirect logic is already in place for the `/memento` endpoint. We can reuse that function as it is, but before calling it, we will have to extract value of `Accept-Datetime` header, if present (otherwise default to current time), then convert it to the 14-digit format before calling `showMemento()`. We also need to set some additional response headers such as `Via` when `/timegate` endpoint is hit to advertise the fact that we negotiate the content via `Accept-Datetime` header on TimeGate endpoint.

The way the state names has been implemented is that we need to pass a state names dict as input parameter while initializing an instance of either a ```Factor```, ```TabularCPD``` or ```Inference``` class. If a state names dict is passed, all the methods can work on both internal state names or the custom state names. So we can pass the parameters either way for methods like ```reduce```, ```map_query``` etc. The only case when this will not (and should not) work is when there is no state names dict provided during initialization but we are passing arguments with custom state names that are not consistent with the internal architecture of the state names (as the case above).

> Okay I think the bigger picture question is, should we let raw() be
> default behavior? My vote is apparently No because otherwise I'd have not
> even bothered to make it happen otherwise.
>
> To be more clear: conceptually, input: $input, parameter: value and $output:
> output are different, and do not have to be consistent. That is,
>
>    1. "input"'s value always starts with $ to indicate output from
>    another module;
>    2. "output"'s value always takes a script variable, that is, raw() by
>    default;
>    3. "parameter"'s value will always add quotes to unquoted string. Any
>    script variables that tries to go into "parameter" must use raw. This
>    is to make a clear case between constants (specified strings and numbers)
>    vs. script variables. If we quote strings, it kind of make them stand out
>    from numbers, and unquoted script variables will go un-noticed. This is why
>    I dislike it.
>
> I think this logic is clear. I may have failed to document them well,
> though.

I know this issue was closed quite some time ago, but I have recently migrated 8 separate non-production (staging) Drupal sites to Deis, so I wanted to assure the OP that this _can_ be done.

I feel obligated to say that I have no love whatsoever for either Drupal or WordPress.  From an ops perspective, they were simply _not_ designed to scale horizontally, and that makes them painful to deal with.  The biggest problem I have encountered is that developers (working locally with Drupal, WordPress or other) easily lose site of the fact that production is a _distributed_ environment and depending on local disk for the storage of user-uploaded files (e.g. images) and other similar concerns is tantamount to a cardinal sin in a distributed environment.  And that's the key-- Drupal and WordPress aren't just ill-fitted for containerization, Docker, Deis, etc.  They are ill-fitted for _any_ distributed environment... unless you know what you're doing and hack on your sites quite a bit to make it tenable-- which is what we did with our 8 sites long before Deis was on the scene, simply so that we could scale them horizontally in AWS.

To make this tenable-- we first got all developers working on Drupal sites to understand that production is distributed and educated them on a few caveats that are implied by that.  First: Modules must not be installed via Drupal's admin panel.  In a distributed environment, the server that _serves_ the request to install a module will _have_ that module, but all other servers will not!  We required that any modules they wished to deploy were linked into each site's repository as a git submodule.  Then they can enable and administer the modules as usual using Drupal's admin panel.  Second, user uploaded content shouldn't be stored locally in a "files" directory.  This is harder, but there _are_ modules and libraries available that will allow you to swap local disks for S3 for _all_ user-uploaded content.  It takes some effort to set them up, from what I understand, and performance-wise, it's a bit slower.  The slowness is mainly observable when an administrator or content author browses files (for instance, to select one to embed in a page), because they are actually browsing a bucket, which may have a lot of content in it.  In practice, it seems some of the pain can be taken out of this be making sure you are using the most recent versions of AWS libraries for Drupal, since many performance issues have been tackled in there over time, and impose a sensible "directory" structure on your S3 buckets so that when you browse the bucket via some Drupal module, it's not requesting metadata for hundreds or thousands of files.

Having taken all these steps quite some time ago, these sites moved to Deis with no trouble whatsoever.  So... it's possible.

As an interesting related note, zsh provides this "helpful" prompt when you `rm *`:
```shell

zsh: sure you want to delete all the files in /tmp/rm-test [yn]? y

Link for full doc on [RM_STAR_SILENT](http://zsh.sourceforge.net/Doc/Release/Options.html#index-RMSTARSILENT).
Personally, I find that behavior is usually annoying, but very occasionally makes you take another look at what you've entered.
The adjacent option, `RM_STAR_WAIT`, is clingy and overprotective, by contrast.

I would sooner do what ZSH does and apply the prompt (with available override flag) on all paths ending in a raw `*`, rather than trying to replicate the current CLI behavior.

IMHO all this `CONDA_FORCE_32BIT` stuff needs to go away and be replaced with being able to have an arbitrary platform declared for each environment (internally the platforms are usually called subdirs).

Why shouldn't someone on Windows be able to install and try an environment containing Linux software under Windows Subsystem for Linux, or a Linux or macOS user be able to do the same using Wine? 

Sure many things won't work well and we'd probably not have time to look into such esoteric setups, but internally we need to be able to do this anyway to support cross-compilation.

I accept the criticism. The function is really a subfunction of `package_cache()`, which reads the contents of `pkgs/` and connects the packages their to their full URLs and channels. 

It is part of the larger effort make sure `conda` can handle packages with identical names from different channels. For instance, previous versions of `conda` did not behave properly if you downloaded package `A.tar.bz` from channel 1 into the package cache, and then attempted to install `A.tar.bz` from channel 2 instead.

We made a choice not to change the on-disk format of the package cache when implementing the channel collision handling. I think a ground-up reimplementation of package caching, that allows mutliple packages with the same name _on disk_, would result in a simpler implementation.

In https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/issues/575#issuecomment-243302507, we got feedback that we the output type is set to `json`, commands should always return valid JSON and **empty string is not valid JSON** (https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/issues/794#issuecomment-252692389).

The PR went in earlier this week (https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/pull/1854).

So now we return the string `null` for commands that prev. returned nothing, `[] `for empty lists, `{}` for empty dictionaries, etc. according to https://docs.python.org/3/library/json.html#json.JSONEncoder.

We could change it so only if the result is an empty list we return `[]` and otherwise return empty but then we wouldn't be strictly following JSON.

Thanks for the issue :)

We've definitely had some thoughts about image manipulation at commit time -- I happen to know that yelp has some closed source pre-commit hooks for optimizing pngs, jpegs, and svgs -- but unfortunately they haven't open sourced them yet.  @chriskuehl @thebostik

The linked article is pretty easy to implement in pre-commit as well -- there's a couple choices:

### as a `script` hook assuming that `pngcrush` is installed

Something like this:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# pngcrush only takes one argument at a time, use `-ow` to in-place modify
xargs -I{} <<< "$@" pngcrush -rem allb -brute -reduce -ow {}
```
With a configuration like:

```yaml
-   id: pngcrush-system
    name: pngcrush
    language: script
    entry: pngcrush.sh
    types: [png]
```

### as a `docker` hook (probably more universally useful

(use the same script as above)

```dockerfile
FROM ubuntu:xenial
RUN apt-get update && \
    DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
        pngcrush && \
    apt-get clean
ADD pngcrush.sh /usr/local/bin/pngcrush.sh
```

With a configuration file like

```yaml
-   id: pngcrush
    name: pngcrush
    language: docker
    entry: pngcrush.sh
    types: [png]
```

### as a local hook

This *could* even be implemented without a repository, it's not pretty and not super portable but:

```yaml
-   repo: local
    hooks:
    -   id: pngcrush
        name: pngcrush
        language: system
        entry: bash -c 'xargs -I{} <<< "$@" pngcrush -rem allb -brute -reduce -ow {}'
        types: [png]
```

### _Note: I haven't tested any of these, they're just some (hopefully) useful sketches to get you started on how you'd go about implementing this_

This should certainly be feasible with nbgrader, for the most part.

> central academic team create assessment notebook(s) for manual grading and with marking guide annoations;

You'll want to set up a shared notebook server (not a full JupyterHub---just the notebook which is running at a URL that multiple people can access). Then your central academic team can use that notebook server to create the instructor versions of your assignments. Alternately, you can have the team all install nbgrader separately and contribute notebooks by e.g. pushing to a git repository that hosts them. But you will need the shared server anyway for grading, so it might be easier just to do that.

> central academic team export notebook files stripped of marking guide notes that can be published as static files on VLE and downloaded by students from there; annotated notebooks with marking guidance are exported for download by markers from tutor area of VLE.

nbgrader doesn't have any explicit way to have rubrics/marking guidance, but what I've done in the past is to put marking guidelines in the "solution" regions. So these regions will get removed when running `nbgrader assign`, and so the students won't see them, and you can distribute the source version of the notebook to markers so they can see the guidelines.

> student to enter an ID number and name into their notebook assessed notebooks, run the notebooks, zip them and then submit the zip file as an attachment through our legacy online assessment handling tools;
>
> tutor/markers to use our legacy assessment handling tool to download zip files containing submissions from each of their students; (we don't control the zip structure of the outer layer which is of the form courseCode/assignmentCode/tutorID/region/studentID)
>
> markers point grading tool at downloaded student files and load them into grading tool showing assignment/student listing;

The `nbgrader zip_collect` command was designed to help with exactly this! See: http://nbgrader.readthedocs.io/en/stable/user_guide/managing_assignment_files_manually.html

> markers manually mark and comment on notebook assignments;

As I mentioned earlier, for multiple graders you'll need a shared notebook server running the formgrader which all the graders can access.

I've created a [cookiecutter](https://github.com/jupyterlab/mimerender-cookiecutter) for generating renderer extensions for both classic notebook and JupyterLab. @jhamrick, I think we're talking past each other, because that division works for distinguishing between editing and command modes. But before what was discussed was the notion of selection being an implicit mark that is differentiated from the notion of what I think you're calling a sticky mark. 

I thought that focused was the term for when you are in edit mode in a particular cell.

In the previous release there was the notion of selecting multiple cells, and so something could be selected and not focused (because you can select multiple cells, but you cannot be _focused_ on multiple cells for the purposes of editing those multiple cells, unless — in the emacs analogy — it's allowing multiple cursors). 

So there are actually 8 states:

Focused/in edit mode (with "sticky" mark)
Focused/in edit mode (without "sticky" mark)
Selected/in command mode (with "sticky" mark)
Selected/in command mode (without "sticky" mark)
Not selected and not focused, in command mode (with "sticky" mark)
Not selected and not focused, in command mode (without "sticky" mark)
Not selected and not focused, in edit mode in another cell (with "sticky" mark)
Not selected and not focused, in edit mode in another cell (without "sticky" mark)

What I was taking as given was that operations could only be applied while in command mode (I think this is implicit in all the discussion, and I have no quibble with that). 

But when in command mode, one could have a selection on many contiguous cells and one could have marks on any subset of the cells. What I was suggesting is the ability to (at least) be able to toggle between the selected cells as the target of actions and the marked cells as the target of actions without losing either the marks or the selection when that toggle occurs(I.e., you can toggle back and forth). The UI indicator for the currently active target set is an open question (and I am open minded as to what that should look like). 

Basically, what's inevitably going to happen in the examples @jhamrick gives in an issue elsewhere(https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/issues/665#issuecomment-151339333) will be cases someone applies an operation to the wrong target set, and it should be possible to undo that operation, to and to immediately return the set of focuses to the place they were before, easily toggle to the correct target set and then apply the operation again. If you don't have some way of encoding that information so as to make it easy to undo to the state where both the marks and selections are preserved, switch targets, and redo; people(read: this includes at least me) will be less likely to use marks as opposed to the contiguous selection mechanism because then I'll at least know where all of the changes were made when misfires occur and apply to the wrong target set (which currently is being implicitly decided by…I'm not sure what?).

This function computes the correlation for a pair of variants. IIRC, the fill value is used in the case where the variance within either variant in a pair is zero, i.e., the variant has the same genotype for all individuals. At least that's what's supposed to happen. Maybe there is some other numerical condition that can result in a nan even if variance is non-zero for both variants. I've been analyzing the outstanding issues, and in fact we're very close to be able to call this 2.0. There are several things in the pipeline that would be nice to have, but only a few represent truly incompatible changes.

* #375 discusses changes to the Model API that need to be decided before release. I think the outcome of the main discussion there is that we could clean up the Model methods a little bit and provide a mixin that implements full `MutableMapping` functionality. (I believe methods like `items()` are rarely used.)

* #372 introduced the notion of unset, or undefined, fields that don't have a value. (This is to be able to distinguish `None` from missing.) The feature entails pretty significant changes that have been hashed out here to an extent, but very few users have actually commented on anything. Mostly it's simply additional functionality, but the new behavior of default values is a notable breaking change (to the better, I hope).

Previously I mentioned error handling. I think I've now thought it through, and it seems that, unless there's a reason to fundamentally change the error message dictionary structure, implementing formal error IDs to accompany the current human-readable strings is a fairly straightforward additive change and hence not a blocker.

@JBKahn I'm curious, how does the custom error dictionary differ from the standard one?

Schematics v1 does have a known issue with `ListType` errors: there's no index to the offending item. But this is fixed in v2; there's an example at the end of #369.

I preferred composition in this case, to make the split more clear. Also because the Model is already complicated enough with metaclass behaviour and properties, I opted not to overload it with more attributes (that could conflict with user-defined properties). It also makes the interaction with SQLAlchemy Base easier to understand.

The Model responsibilities now is to encapsulate API usage, the schema and data state.

Hi, this is a good use case, we don't have a property for that (maybe we should) but you can achieve it right now with model validators (it does require using a private accessor):

    class Player(Model):
        id = IntType()
        name = StringType()

        def validate_id(self, context, value):
            if self._data.valid.get('id'):
                raise ValidationError('Cannot change id')


Sample usage:

    p1 = Player({'id': 4})
    p1.validate()
    p1.id = 3
    p1.name = 'Douglas'
    p1.validate()

    schematics.exceptions.DataError: {"id": ["Cannot change id"]}

Python2 is good, but there is still a problem in python3.

This is in the line `isinstance(x, int) or isinstance(x, np.int_)` used in sampyl.discrete_uniform.  The value is not an instance of the wrapped int_ type because it was not create by the wrapper, so it is only the first check that has an effect.  In numpy for python2, numpy.int64 has int as a base class so it returns true, but surprisingly to me in numpy for python3 this is not the case.

I am not sure what you can do about this case - it may have to be fixed in sampyl with a more robust integral check like `isinstance(x,numbers.Integral)`

It's not possible out of the box because it's better for the library to expose one simple and flexible way to access the computation's output: via the _generator_ returned by `stream()`. From there, you're free to consume it however you want. In your case, you can easily pull it into a queue:

```python
import queue

The typical/suggested strategy is to publish messages to localhost, not try to use nsqlookupd to find where to publish messages to. (That's for nsq consumers.)

The simplest way to publish messages is with the nsqd http publish endpoint. You can use any http client library you like. It's very simple.

It is possible to use an nsq library and the tcp-binary-protocol to publish messages, but it may be awkward or tricky depending on what you are trying to do.

This is a breaking change.

This is a surprisingly tricky goal. There are two methods which Python might use to cast an object to a string: `__str__` and `__repr__`. There it lots of literature on the topic already, such as [this Stack Overflow thread](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/1436703/difference-between-str-and-repr-in-python) and the official documentation on [`__str__`](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#object.__str__) and [`__repr__`](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#object.__repr__). Thus, I won't try to provide a full explanation of the two methods here.

Done reading the linked-to material? Cool, cool. In the context of this bug, one important distinction between `__str__` and `__repr__` is what happens when a container is stringified. Check this out:

```python
>>> class Foo(object):
...   def __str__(self):
...     return 'Yellow Submarine'
... 
>>> str(Foo())
'Yellow Submarine'
>>> repr(Foo())
'<__main__.Foo object at 0x7f9ba3f86438>'
>>> str([Foo(), Foo()])
'[<__main__.Foo object at 0x7f9ba3f86438>, <__main__.Foo object at 0x7f9ba3f864a8>]'
```

As you can see, Python calls `repr` on each element in an iterable, even if the elements of the iterable support `str`.

So - should we just define `__repr__` on class `nailgun.entity_mixins.Entity`? I don't think so, no. The difficulty is that `__rep__` is supposed to provide an unambiguous representation of an object. [For example](http://stackoverflow.com/a/19597196):

```python
>>> import datetime
>>> today = datetime.datetime.now()
>>> str(today)
'2012-03-14 09:21:58.130922'
>>> repr(today)
'datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 14, 9, 21, 58, 130922)'
```

Thus, we should only define `nailgun.entity_mixins.Entity.__repr__` if we also define `nailgun.config.ServerConfig.__repr__`. And the result of all this will be some horrifically long string like this:

```python
nailgun.entities.MyEntity(nailgun.config.ServerConfig('https://sat.example.com', ('username', 'password'), '6.0.8', False), id=5, name='foo', description='bar')
```

And just imagine what it would look like if `MyEntity` referenced any other entity objects!

A more realistic goal is to simply define `nailgun.entity_mixins.Entity.__str__`. Doing so will still mean that we will get terminal output like this:

```python
>>> print(location.config_template)
[
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c0be90>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c0b910>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c16050>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c16310>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c165d0>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c16890>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c16b50>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c16e10>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c19110>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c193d0>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c19690>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c19950>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c19c10>
]
```

Defining `__str__` will still provide a benefit when examining entity objects one at a time:

```python
>>> print(location.config_template[0])
<ConfigTemplate: 18>
```

New approach online: Instead of calculating reporter values directly we can use `map()` to "attach" functions to agents. This returns a generator that uses lazy evaluation to calculate its values. 

This means the downside for non string based agents mentioned above is circumvented and we have the result
![no_overhead](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/13568919/44582374-c155d080-a7a1-11e8-8c8b-9695bea35062.png)
for ANY reporter function.

However, the work is only shifted to the `get_agent_vars_dataframe()` method. If we evaluate this function at the end of a model run, the total runtime will be the same as in the updated data collection above (again, with the potential fast path for attribute reporters). 

But if we are not interested in the collected data (for example for interactive runs) the collect method no longer slows down model runs. 

Two things to discuss:
    1) In the current implementation accessing the generator functions "empties" them, meaning we can only call `get_agent_vars_dataframe()` once. This is easy to prevent, but it could be a desirable side-effect to free up memory (e.g. dumping the data every step into a database). Don't know which version to prefer although I guess the emptying will be unexpected for most users (and so should be prevented)
    2) We could use the same approach for model reporters.

@hpk42 Ok now I'm confused. 
As far as I can tell this is already allowed? When `_HookCaller.__call__()` is invoked there's never any checking against the spec. This is why I added warnings in #34.

So right now **all hookspec args are optional**. That is, a `hookimpl` only must declare a subset of them. The only case you'll get an error is when an impl has more arguments then what's provided by the caller.

>Hook implementations then need to be able to accept such an optional argument and detect if it was provided from the caller or not. At the same time a hook implementation might want to work with both the old and the new hookspec and thus only optionally accept the optional argument.

@hpk42 yes this is the tricky part and would be the reason to use a `kwarg` in the `hookimpl`. But what I've been trying to say is that `hookimpl` could always do this.
You're perfectly allowed to define a `hookimpl` like the following:
```python
@hookimpl
def myhook(doggy, kitty, reason="The default reason"):
    ...
```
All that needs to change is the logic in `_MultiCall.execute()`:
- any `kwarg` values defined in the `hookimpl` (in this case ^ `reason`) should receive values from the caller **if provided** (so we **don't** error like in the `args` case)

Ok I'll try to explain why the tests should be inside the package that is being tested. I learned this from far more experienced pythonistas than me, who explained to me that this is the way GOD intended.

When you are working on project, you can either do like django and stick all files related to the project in a single egg. Or you can do like [plone](https://github.com/plone/Products.Archetypes/tree/master/Products/Archetypes/tests) and [pyramid](https://github.com/Pylons/pyramid/tree/master/pyramid/tests) and split your big project over discrete eggs with a single responsibility.

Suppose you choose the last method. Now you are working on lots of eggs at the same time. You have them in your virtualenv as develop eggs or in a buildout or whatever. What happens to all the top level `tests` folders? If I made them a package by including a __init__.py I've got a problem, because all these eggs have a package with the same name. If I don't include an __init__.py they are not packages and I can not refer to them in any other way then by their path.

When working with a project split up this way, it is a very nice thing to use nose to run tests. You can run something like:

```
nose project.first project.second project.third #options omitted
```

That will run all the unit tests inside those packages and the doctests as well. So I can work on many eggs and test them all at once, no testrunner writing needed.

Now the truth is, that in this specific case, this reasoning is not valid, since I made the tests dependent on the settings in the sandbox. If I did manage to make the tests context (settings) independent, it would be the right thing to do. (Note that you CAN split a large django project into separate apps/eggs and then you need to structure the tests like that.)

That being said, I kindly ask to be excused, when I leave the factoring out of the test suite to you.

@hjc4869, if you don't want to specify all of the inputs to ILLink by using the task in your .csproj, you should be able to do this by inserting your own target that removes these assemblies from LinkerRootAssemblies, like this:

```xml
<Target Name="UnrootAssemblies" AfterTargets="_ComputeLinkerRootAssemblies">
  <ItemGroup>
    <LinkerRootAssemblies Remove="Assembly.Name.Without.Dll.Extension" />
  </ItemGroup>
</Target>
```
This relies on the private target _ComputeLinkerRootAssemblies, so consider this unsupported. :) We should probably make the default behavior more configurable to make this easier in the future.

@DavidMuller Glad I could help, though I will clarify that as far as I know, `rrule` does not actually currently use date arithmetic, just that the effect is the same, because the original `tzinfo` object attached to `dtstart` is simply copied to each new object.

The real problem is that the timezone localization that `pytz` calculates and attaches via the `localize` method is calculated only at the beginning and nothing is triggering its recalculation (as compared with the `tzfile`, which calculates the timezone upon request rather than on creation of the date).

As a general rule I try not to test the private interface, so I prefer not to test what `_filename ` is set to as that is an implementation detail. It's more important to test the public effects of this than the internal representation.

I think for other tzinfo types this is tested by seeing the effect it has on the `repr`.

I think we just need to figure out what the effort / reward trade-off is here. I'm willing to spend a bit of time figuring this out because it's very useful as a general rule, but if in the end it turns out that the options are 1) write or maintain a complicated benchmarking suite or 2) provide *something* that is more likely to be misleading than helpful, I'd say we just give up on benchmarking until we can free-load on someone who has already done 1. (Which may be `asv` and we're just running it wrong).

In the end it may be that we set up `asv` and use it *only* on Tom's `asv-runner` as a more-or-less stable "most accurate" measurement, and then *also* add `pytest-benchmark` or `pytest-profiling` tests for fast iteration while trying to make performance improvements, though obviously it would be easiest if these benchmarks were the same.

> Can you explain: `while not self.updates_thread_stopping:`

That's a mistake actually. Should be `updates_thread_stopped` (simply the opposite of `updates_thread_running`).

> and `if not self.waiting_receive:`

After every `.send()` a `.receive()` is made. So the `.send()` method sets this flag to `True` so the updates thread can't, in theory, try receiving the updates while we want to receive the request result. After the `.receive()` reads the result of the invoked result, this flag is set to `False`, so the updates thread can continue its job.

> So may be it is the case when it comes to races?

Maybe that's the reason, maybe the flag set should be inside the lock.

I think a good example would be how the `Marker` and `Popup` classes currently work. `Tooltip` should work similarly to `Popup`. I'll add some pointers and suggestions here:

The crux here is to add a line in the `Tooltip` template where the Leaflet tooltip object is added to its parent object, whether that's a geojson thing or a marker or anything:

`{{ this._parent.get_name() }}.bindTooltip({{ this.get_name() }})`

This means that in the template you must first create a Leaflet tooltip object:

`var {{ this.get_name() }} = L.tooltip();`

If you move your template to `Tooltip`, note that you no longer have to use `this.tooltip.whatever` but can simplify it to `this.whatever`.

In `GeoJson` you can still check the validity of the fields in the `Tooltip` object that is passed, but after that you shouldn't store it in `self.tooltip`, but instead add it as a child: `self.add_child(tooltip)`.

`Popup` doesn't have a `text` argument, but instead accepts html, with a flag to allow for the string to be parsed. I'm not entirely happy with this because users may assume to just be able to pass a string without checking that it should be valid html and then the map silently failing. But  that's a discussion for somewhere else. Maybe be consistent here and use it in the same way.

`` \_\_init__(self, html=None, parse_html=False, ...`

You mentioned adapting `Popup` to work with `GeoJson` fields as well, that sounds interesting. More reason to make these classes similar, so if a user understands one they can use the other as well.

By the way, why do you need `-t` option? I do not know how it is on Mac OS X, but on linux `-t` option does *not* specify template. @tinproject, thanks for stating this clearly, in a perhaps less convoluted way than I did.

I agree with everything you said.

The only I part wasn't totally convinced about is why we need to decouple the schema from the model. I use the schema to decouple API I/O from model objects, but I don't see the need for a schema field with a name that differs from both the model and the API. From an architectural point of view, I mean. From a practical perspective, I just identified a use case. `setattr` allows you to set attributes with names that are not legal Python variable names (such as 'my-attribute'). I don't see a reason for doing so, but assuming you're dealing with such an object, having the `attribute` parameter comes in handy. For this, plus compatibility with existing code, I agree we might as well keep `attribute`.

The new parameter sort of overlaps with `attribute`, as 

```python
my_field = Field(attribute='field')
```

would be equivalent to

```python
field = Field(key='my_field')
```

but it does not matter IMHO. Keeping both shouldn't be an issue in terms of code maintenance. I'm more worried about the `load_from` / `dump_to` parameters. They lead to confusion, and undefined cases (see an example here: https://github.com/marshmallow-code/apispec/issues/178), so these are the one I'd like to get rid of.

Regarding the name of the new attribute, I think `name` is as unintuitive as `key`. I like `field`. My only concern: the elements of the schemas are also called "fields", and this parameter does not expect a schema field name but the name of a field in the serialiazed representation. Yet, I don't think this is blocking and it doesn't take 20 lines of documentation to make it clear, so `field` would be fine for me.

Yes, this is correct. This is a helper function for computing various ranking-related evaluation scores where you need to know the position (in the full ranked list for any given user) of the test interactions.

BPR always chooses one negative example per positive example.

WARP also attempts to find one negative example per positive example, but sampled negative examples that are ranked lower than the positive examples are discarded. The algorithm then tries again up to `max_sampled` times; if that fails, it just moves on to the next positive without performing a gradient step.

@JianGoForIt: I see, checking the PyTorch SGD code, they default `dampening` to 0 (https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/master/torch/optim/sgd.py#L52) where the original Torch code defaults `dampening` to `momentum`: https://github.com/torch/optim/blob/master/sgd.lua#L35. Sorry for the red herring.

The docstring in PyTorch [points out another difference](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/master/torch/optim/sgd.py#L31-L45), though.
Lasagne has:
* velocity := velocity * momentum - eta * grad
* param := param + velocity

PyTorch has:
* velocity := velocity * momentum - grad
* param := param + eta * velocity

Again, not sure if that's critical, given that the Equation 1 in the paper matches Lasagne's update rule (and possibly TensorFlow's as well, haven't checked).

Also thanks for the clarification on spatial softmax.I'm aiming to use it in reinforcement learning, as in [https://arxiv.org/pdf/1504.00702.pdf](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1504.00702.pdf), and I did not realize there was another, more common usage of the term (!).  So, the math I'm trying to do for the expectations is like: ``expected_h = sum_{i,j}(h_{i} * z_{i,j})``,  where h is the 1-D position, z is the 2-D softmax, separate for each channel.  I checked the math on the dot-sum operations using numpy, and it seems to be correct there (both when ``h==w`` and when ``h!=w``).  Appears I may be a bit off-topic, then, but here is the newer code anyway (it builds, but I haven't tested learning with it just yet):

```python
class SpatialSoftmaxLayer(L.Layer):

    def __init__(self, incoming, temperature=1., learn_temperature=False,
            name='spatial_softmax'):
        super().__init__(incoming, name=name)
        if learn_temperature:
            self.temperature = self.add_param(LI.Constant(temperature), (1,),
                name="temperature", regularizable=False)
        else:
            self.temperature = temperature

    def get_output_shape_for(self, input_shape):
        assert len(input_shape) == 4
        return input_shape[0], input_shape[1], 3

    def get_output_for(self, input, **kwargs):
        """ Performs 2D spatial softmax, returns expected x & y coordinates and
        the max value out of the softmax (to give a hint how sharp it is)"""
        n, c, h, w = input.shape
        input_2D = T.reshape(input, (n * c, h * w))
        softmax_2D = T.nnet.softmax(input_2D / self.temperature)
        softmax_4D = T.reshape(softmax_2D, (n, c, h, w))
        # Construct ranges from -1 to 1.
        pos_h = (2 * T.arange(h, dtype=floatX) - (h - 1)) / (h - 1)
        pos_w = (2 * T.arange(w, dtype=floatX) - (w - 1)) / (w - 1)
        expected_h = T.dot(pos_h, softmax_4D).sum(axis=2)
        expected_w = T.dot(softmax_4D, pos_w).sum(axis=2)
        softmax_3D = T.reshape(softmax_2D, (n, c, h * w))
        z = T.max(softmax_3D, axis=2)
        output = T.stack([expected_h, expected_w, z], axis=2)
        return output
```

Thank you for your input! A related issue is #637. And I think this was discussed on lasagne-users not long ago, but I cannot find it right now.

The main objections were that we cannot remember all initializers. While it's easy for stateless callables as provided in `lasagne.init`, and possible for numpy arrays (at possibly nontrivial memory costs), it simply won't work for shared variables or stateful callables. So we cannot provide a general solution in Lasagne.

Your solution proposes to check for the initialization specification to be a `lasagne.init.Initializer` descendent, which is a good heuristic for stateless callables, but not a guarantee, and which would exclude any custom initialization functions from being remembered.
For your use case, custom initialization functions and numpy arrays do not play a role, but for other use cases (as #637) they do. It might give users some headaches if Lasagne remembered initializers out-of-the-box, but then only did so for very specific cases.

I think a better solution might be providing some kind of hook that allows you to do the bookkeeping yourself. You can already hook in to the process by monkey-patching `Layer.add_param()` or `lasagne.utils.create_param()`, but we may consider adding some better-supported way.
To detail, what you could do right now would be monkey-patching `lasagne.utils.create_param()` to call the original `create_param()`, but then set `param.tag.initializer` to the original specification before returning it. This would attach the initializer directly to the shared parameter variable.

Thanks for this @irvind! One small note. =)

I think that the right thing to do for now might actually be refuse to ever *decode* these meta-characters; i.e. `URL.from_text('%25%2F').asIRI()` → `URL.from_text('%25%2F')`.

Right now, `.path` and `.query` are literal text as they appear in the URI itself.  This makes sense as a data structure, although it is somewhat limiting.  It's also a necessary piece of structure to preserve, because the conversion to "decoded" characters is not information-preserving: since a percent-encoded sequence might represent an invalid UTF-8 sequence of octets, for example.  But it also means that reserved characters can never appear in them without escaping.

The thing to do for segments that encode slashes, question marks and percent signs, I believe, would be to have a separate attribute without the exception-raising restriction that we currently have; `decoded_path` for a punching-bag name suggestion.

OK, so I found a clear bug here.

```
# Create a prepared application
a=prepareGaudiExec('DaVinci','v42r1')
a.options='/home/hep/egede/minimal2.py'
a.prepare()

# Now assign this application to two jobs
j = Job(application=a, backend=Dirac())
j2 = Job(application=a, backend=Dirac())

# Now submit
j.submit()
INFO     submitting job 35
INFO     job 35 status changed to "submitting"
INFO     Job 35's application has already been prepared.
INFO     Uploading file '/tmp/__conf-38d4088a-9aba-4293-b55b-e260ce38fabc.tgz' to 'PIC-USER' as '/lhcb/user/e/egede/GangaInputFile/Job_35/__conf-38d4088a-9aba-4293-b55b-e260ce38fabc.tgz'
:
:

j2.submit()
INFO     submitting job 36
INFO     job 36 status changed to "submitting"
INFO     Job 36's application has already been prepared.
INFO     Uploading file '/tmp/__conf-38d4088a-9aba-4293-b55b-e260ce38fabc.tgz' to 'PIC-USER' as '/lhcb/user/e/egede/GangaInputFile/Job_36/__conf-38d4088a-9aba-4293-b55b-e260ce38fabc.tgz'
:
```
It can be seen here that we attempt to upload the shared file twice to Dirac (and from the same /tmp location). If these two submissions happen at the same time, then it will fail.

The bug is that the information about that the shared file object is already uploaded is kept as a property of the application object (```app.uploadedInput```) where it should instead be a property that is stored in the shared file area. This can prevent that the file is uploaded twice. Now to protect against the race condition, an exclusive lock will need to be placed on the upload operation (in the same way as there is an exclusive lock on the prepare step for GaudiExec).

Just a quick update and to document what we have, so far with Webrecorder and the [experimental Dat support](https://blog.webrecorder.io/2018/08/02/webrecorder-dat-integration.html), we've 
been using:

```
- <coll id>
    |
    |- dat.json
    |
    |- /warcs/
    |
    |- /indexes/
    |
    |- /metadata/metadata.yaml
```

The metadata directory contains a single file, `metadata.yaml`, which includes all Webrecorder specific metadata necessary for the collection (pages, lists, bookmarks). For a prototype, it ended up being simpler to put everything in one file, which includes things like collection size as well as the page/bookmark entrypoints. For interoperability, it probably makes more sense to put entrypoints into an `entrypoints` directory.

The `dat.json` is added when converting to Dat to conform to Dat requirements.
A custom (`type: ["webarchive", "webrecorder-webarchive"]`) is added to the dat.json to indicate that its a web archive collection.

Webrecorder has been using `warcs` instead of `archives` as that was more concrete, but perhaps `capture` would be an even better term? (and pywb still uses `archives` for now, so need to fix that). Any thoughts on `warcs` vs `archives` vs `capture` @despens ? We should probably settle on one one to standardize on!

Still refining this but will try to propose a more unified dir structure, especially before making the Dat support more widespread as that's been a key motivator in trying to have a unified dir structure.

Ideally, there could be a generic web archive collection that can be shared via Dat, as well as tool-specific metadata that can be layered on top. Though, a tricky aspect is that if Webrecorder/pywb are the only tools to use this, it is harder to tease out the generic vs more tool-specific properties.

Any additional feedback welcome!

Note that I don't think (1) is ultimately an issue. It is merely a scaling factor and has no effect on most trend analysis. That said, I agree it is usually normalized by number density. 

More generally, I find this to be a very poor and overly complicated implementation of a RDF. You do not actually need to do the whole complicated dist_rdf[dist] += 1 (first off, a better implementation of that would use a Counter). You simply need to create a sequence of all distances with no counting at all, then use numpy.histogram to bin all the distances by dist, i.e., somehting like

```
# Use get_all_neighbors instead of get_neighbors
# Compile the distances into a list.
# Concatenate the distances
# Create histogram of distances (numpy will even normalize it properly for you if you specify normed=True)
```

I think this will accomplish the RDF in less than 5 lines, and will probably be 10x or more faster.

It is impossible for a testing software play well on both sides dev/production, the assumptions and premises are different. In this thread we can see that for development version we must trigger not working behavior and other destructive actions that will ensure a given development version is production ready.

On the other hand, for production a given installation can be health check by running a set of scripts or tests that must never do anything silly, like dropping database. Also the cleanup should be reinforced, but it should be careful tested because if some wrong id is used it will lead on data loss.

Pulp Smash initial design was to be a tool that exercises Pulp on its development environment and gives a positive or negative feedback to say if that development version is production ready. This does not mean that on production it will not raise issues. With that said, my concern here is to, if a production health check is needed a different set of tests should be created ensuring the constraints for the production check, Pulp Smash tools like its API and CLI client can be used for this.

In terms of roadmap, having a health check for production is not something listed on the top priority items because Pulp Smash still lacking of coverage for some areas in order to be able to say if a development version is production ready, maybe when we get there we can focus on providing production check.

With all that said, what we can do for this PR is to adequate it to run on development environment which is the only one environment where Pulp Smash is run right now. Production check should be thought better and careful created later, if one destructive operation goes hidden then it will be a massive loss, Pulp Smash must not be run on production environment until then.

Dear @yfilali , thank you for your thoughtful response.

Indeed, using `graphene-django` it is really easy to expose a Django model to the GraphQL API and keep them in sync.

The reason I asked the question is because we have already implemented quite a big GraphQL schema on Graphene and only a small part of GraphQL types we have are created with `graphene-django`. It does work quite well, but it is hard to capture the schema reading these dozens of Python classes. That lead me to the thought that it would be nice to split the GraphQL schema declaration from the resolvers implementation. I am still not sure, but I see [having schema statically declared in the language-agnostic manner](https://dev-blog.apollodata.com/5-benefits-of-static-graphql-queries-b7fa90b0b69a) is quite an interesting option.

In other words, I am still not sure that combining schema definition and schema implementation (implementation of the resolvers hierarchy) is a good approach for writing GraphQL API.

I would love to hear other opinions.

I haven't tested this thoroughly, but the follow seems to work with graphene 2.0.
```Python
class DjangoFilterField(Field):
    '''
    Custom field to use django-filter with graphene object types (without relay).
    '''

    def __init__(self, _type, fields=None, extra_filter_meta=None,
                 filterset_class=None, *args, **kwargs):
        _fields = _type._meta.filter_fields
        _model = _type._meta.model
        self.of_type = _type
        self.fields = fields or _fields
        meta = dict(model=_model, fields=self.fields)
        if extra_filter_meta:
            meta.update(extra_filter_meta)
        self.filterset_class = get_filterset_class(filterset_class, **meta)
        self.filtering_args = get_filtering_args_from_filterset(
            self.filterset_class, _type)
        kwargs.setdefault('args', {})
        kwargs['args'].update(self.filtering_args)
        super().__init__(List(_type), *args, **kwargs)

    @staticmethod
    def list_resolver(manager, filterset_class, filtering_args, root, info, *args, **kwargs):
        filter_kwargs = {k: v for k,
                         v in kwargs.items() if k in filtering_args}
        qs = manager.get_queryset()
        qs = filterset_class(data=filter_kwargs, queryset=qs).qs
        return qs

    def get_resolver(self, parent_resolver):
        return partial(self.list_resolver, self.of_type._meta.model._default_manager,
                       self.filterset_class, self.filtering_args)

```
Key changes I have made are:
1. Tracking type as self.type now returns List instead of the type of list it is.
2. Changing params passed list_resolver.

@jleclanche: Thanks for looking at this.  I'm not sure what "...the same transaction as what's happening before" refers to?

Here are a lot more details about the problem.

Our original code used django-auth-toolkit 0.12.0. In that version, if you do the following:
1. get a refresh token with an access token.
2. revoke the access token.
3. call `create_token_response` with this refresh token.  
We would get a 401.

When I upgraded to django-auth-toolkit 1.1.2, the behavior had changed, due to an issue I will describe in more detail.

For reference for my description below, here are the relevant lines in `oauthlib` in `validate_token_request`:
https://github.com/oauthlib/oauthlib/blob/v2.1.0/oauthlib/oauth2/rfc6749/grant_types/refresh_token.py#L109-L116

**The problem:**
In `oauthlib: validate_token_request`, the refresh token is currently being returned as valid by `request_validator.validate_refresh_token`, but when it moves on to `request_validator.get_original_scopes`, it can't find the access token because it has been revoked and returns an error, that in turn becomes a 500 error.

What is probably warranted is an integration test that shows the whole flow between `create_token_response`, through `oauthlib`, calling back to the relevant `oauth2_provider` code.

@pvanhorn Thank you for a precise explanation. I think all discussion here are related to technical and architectural aspects of the stack and none of us receives it negatively :). Please forgive us if not all our statements are clear but we are not native English speakers so something can be lost in translation. 

I think hubertmis & LuDuda did not mean that you had suggested that Openthread should not offer the ability to perform CSMA/CA that is compliant with 802.15.4. I think they meant that it would be nice if the stack provided a native support for SW assisted CSMA-CA procedure for platforms that don't support it in hardware. An argument was that Nordic platform is not the only platform within OpenThread that requires SW assisted CSMA-CA, so adding a native support for this to the stack could benefit two platfoms already present (Nordic and cc2538) and possibly more in the future.

On the other hand I understand your endeavour to keep the stack architecture clean and as a result reluctance to add specialized features to general modules. 

To move on with this issue our decission is to deal with it within the platform specific code as you suggested. As you accuretly noticed it adds a little bit more complication on our side (indeed optimaly otPlatformAlarm and otPlatformUsecAlarm would rely on the same platform resource) but as an adventage this solution will not interfere with the code already in place. I think that as an intermediate stage we can use different HW resources for otPlatformAlarm and otPlatformUsecAlarm, which will be much simpler and faster, and than optimize to use only one HW resource for both. Kind regards, Krzysztof.

This will build your model two times.

OR
- You can reuse variables by setting scopes:
```
def model(data, isUsingDropout):
    net = tflearn.fully_connected(data, num_hidden1, activation='relu', scope='fc1')
    net = tflearn.dropout(net, kDropoutProbability)
    net = tflearn.fully_connected(net, num_hidden2, activation='relu'. scope='fc2')
    net = tflearn.dropout(net, kDropoutProbability)
    net = tflearn.fully_connected(net, num_hidden3, activation='relu', scope='fc3')

An initial implementation of inbound handling for Mailgun is available in the [feature_inbound](https://github.com/anymail/django-anymail/tree/feature_inbound) branch.

I'd welcome some real-world testing and feedback on the API. Speak up if you're able to volunteer, and I'll bump whichever ESP you're using to the top of the implementation priorities.

Immediate next step is to get some initial docs in place. If you're itching to get started, inbound works a lot like [tracking webhooks](https://anymail.readthedocs.io/en/stable/sending/tracking/), but:
* The inbound webhook url to give your ESP is ".../inbound/" rather than ".../tracking/". [For Mailgun, you can also use ".../inbound_mime/", because MG changes behavior based on whether the url ends in mime.]
* The signal to listen to is called `inbound` rather than `tracking` (`from anymail.signals import inbound`)
* The `event` param for your signal receiver will be an AnymailInboundEvent, rather than an AnymailTrackingEvent. The inbound event has `event_type` (="inbound"), `timestamp` and `event_id` fields just like a tracking event. It also has a `message` property with all the good stuff.

The `event.message` property is an AnymailInboundMessage, which is a Python email.message.Message subclass, (hopefully) optimized for easy handling of inbound messages. [After some false starts, I settled on this because it can represent pretty much any inbound email message that might arrive. Django's EmailMessage is really designed for outbound, and is much more limited.]

AnymailInboundMessage has convenience accessors so you don't have to worry about navigating a MIME message structure or parsing headers. E.g., `message.html` is the HTML body of the message (if any). `message.from_email.display_name` and `message.from_email.addr_spec` are the parsed display name and email address of the From email header. `message.attachments[0].as_file()` is a Django UploadedFile object for the first attachment. And so on. But all the regular email.message.Message features are there, too, so you can access headers like `message['DKIM-Signature']` or `message.get_all('Received')`. Until there are docs, the [test cases](https://github.com/anymail/django-anymail/blob/feature_inbound/tests/test_inbound.py#L128-L247) have several examples.

Just did a quick test on this branch:
https://github.com/EricCousineau-TRI/pybind11/pull/1/files#diff-2afbeba333b4cc339dabd6a379ea943cR155

Takeaways:
* Generally, static compilation checks for castability between the `Deleter`s of `unique_ptr` will ensure users don't carelessly cast `unique_ptr`s.
* If a user starts mixing `unique_ptr`s and, for whatever reason, uses a nontrivial deleter (something that takes up memory, e.g. `std::function<>` for type-erased destructors) at somewhere along the inheritance chain, they may be in for a nasty surprise if they are not consistent with their API.

The above caveat seems like a rare edge case, and would seem like a general design mistake, so I think this PR is fine as-is; could be addressed at a later point, perhaps with #1161.

Since we need to support Python 2.6, we can only use the comment-style type annotations mentioned in the blog post above (i.e. the ones from the `typing` module). The type-hints-in-function-definitions syntax (such as `def my_function(numbers: List[int]) -> str:` is only available in Python 3.4+.

As for where to start annotating, the blog post above suggests that changes can be gradual and suggests starting with the "core library" first (see here for details: http://blog.zulip.org/2016/10/13/static-types-in-python-oh-mypy/#fullyannotatingalargecodebase). For certbot, this would probably mean starting with:
- `certbot/main.py`
- `certbot/configuration.py` (because `config`, which is an instance of `NamespaceConfig`, is passed around everywhere) 

Also, the blog post suggests standardizing how you're running mypy and sharing that information, as that will allow multiple people to work on this more smoothly!

Just to capture some of my notes so far, so that I don't lose track of them:

It seems like the display code's current complexity comes from a few different sources:
* allowing for both interactive and non-interactive workflows (and, more generally, a spectrum of interactivity, where some information is provided via command-line arguments and some is provided interactively)
* preserving backwards compatibility (i.e., ensuring that adding prompts doesn't add interactivity to commands that were previously non-interactive)
* allowing different prompts to behave differently (e.g., not forcing a standard `wrap` convention on all prompts)
* having an unclear hierarchy (e.g., it's not clear why `yesno` is part of `IDisplay` and not `ops`)
* building on very primative building blocks (i.e., standard libraries + readline)

In addition to the above, the refactoring effort itself is complicated by the need to preserve backwards compatibility both at the Python API layer, for third-party plugins, and in terms of application behavior, to ensure that previously non-interactive commands do not become interactive.

> One concern I have is this except without a raise, which theoretically suggest that the Nginx plugin could be tripped up if a file became unwritable partway through a series of config changes: https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/certbot-nginx/certbot_nginx/parser.py#L219

> However I guess (though I haven't checked closely) that it doesn't matter, because operations like cleanup after challenge completion are actually performed by the reverter, and not by the plugin itself. And not having the raise is valuable until we fix #3205.

Exactly, The reverter works by making a copy of all files that are going to be modified. This should be (and is) done before the files are modified. Even if the modification fails, we have valid backups we can restore to.

In this case, the only function that calls `filedump` is `configurator.NginxConfigurator.save`. Looking at [that method](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/certbot-nginx/certbot_nginx/configurator.py#L511), you'll see we add files that are about to be modified to the checkpoint before calling `filedump`.

In this theoretical case, if the file is unwriteable we might not be able to restore the backup, but I'm not sure what else we could reasonably do here. At the very least, there will be a valid backup on disk that can be used once the file is made writeable again.

### `%run` magic

Basically what I was proposing was that

```
---
%run
statement

[step]
---

would be treated as a normal workflow with a `[global]` section without header. In the mean time, the notebook workflow would consist of all sections so `[step]` would be included. This is different from the current behavior in which `[step]` is excluded because the whole cell does not start with a section header. The new behavior is actually easier to understand because notebook workflow simply contain all sections.

Is category (2) a real thing? Cloudpickle is supposed to be more general. It is true, that pickle "succeeds" at serializing some things that cloudpickle fails at, e.g.,

```python
class Foo(object):
  def __init__(self):
    super

cloudpickle.dumps(Foo)  # PicklingError: Could not pickle object as excessively deep recursion required.
pickle.dumps(Foo)  # b'\x80\x03c__main__\nFoo\nq\x00.'
```

However, this is misleading. Pickle only succeeds because it doesn't capture the class definition (you couldn't unpickle it in another process).

After #550, I think category (3) should more or less be nonexistent. I'm probably wrong about this, but we'll see.

Category (4) is a big problem. E.g., #319.

class foo():

do stuff on actor A

batch_stack.append(t2)
do stuff on actor A

...

tn = new ray.batch()
batch_stack.append(tn)
do stuff on actor A

# checkpoint and clean up
t = batch_stack.pop()
ray.destroy_batch(t) # clean & checkpoint state of actor A
while len(batch_stack) > 0:

Would it be possible to pin something to memory?

For our use-case, least-frequently-used (LFU) may be more effective. Would it be possible to use a similar approach as the Adaptive Replacement Cache (ARC) ([link](http://ieeexplore.ieee.org/abstract/document/1297303/))?

The goal for persisting objects to disk is to prevent having to recreate expensive objects, the assumption being that it is significantly faster to load from disk than it would be to recreate the object. I would be interested to know how Ray would behave in the following scenario:

1.) Load text file from disk, each character in the file is its own remote object.
2.) Memory fills up and objects start getting evicted.
3.) We access an evicted object

Would Ray scan through the file until it reached the correct character? Is the offset of the file for the character stored in the reproduction stack?

We may also have remote objects that were built based on other remote objects, so the cost of recreation/load will be compounded. Would it be possible to choose between evict and persist based on the complexity of recreation or based on whether an evicted object depends on the current object?

Thanks @drejjmit. Looking at the log output it definitely looks to me as though this falls into one of a couple scenarios. The first to me being the most unlikely, and that's a problem with the edge node you are connecting to. I don't believe this is the issue as you're able to stream for exactly 24 hours before the connection is terminated. The second and also less likely one to me is something between you and the edge node is terminating that open connection after 24 hours. Again I think this is unlikely. The third scenario (and one I believe to be the case, but without details from Twitch staff there's no way to know) is that they terminate connections after 24 hours. It seems to me as though that long string value after `/v0/` is most likely unique, and I bet if you ran it again you would see a different value there. The only thing I can think is Twitch is severing these connections and resetting those values after 24 hours (it's what I would do if I worked there to prevent abuse and really long running connections).

At this point everything on Streamlink's end looks good to me, it's doing exactly what it should, retrying until it can no longer reconnect to the URL source it was previously using, which triggers the read timeout and end of the process. Now with that being said we might want to add some sort of 'new' connection retry logic so that it attempts to retrieve a fresh connection instead of connecting to the source it already had. I think this would be a reasonable solution, though I'm not sure how it would impact the recording process because the segment values would change since we're technically establishing a new connection instead of an existing one.

Expanding on that last bullet point: we want to distinguish
```
async def agen():
    with trio.move_on_after(1):
        yield 42
async def caller():
    async for val in agen:
        await trio.sleep(val)
```
from
```
async def agen():
    yield 42
async def caller():
    with trio.move_on_after(1):
        async for val in agen:
            await trio.sleep(val)
```
(the second should interrupt the sleep while the first should not). Luckily, the firstiter hook is called when the coroutine returned by asend() is _constructed_, before the send(None) is performed and thus before any of the async generator code is executed. So if we add asyncgens to `Task._asyncgens` in firstiter, we can have `CancelScope.__enter__` (and any other relevantly context-y things) check each asyncgen's ag_running (works despite bpo-32526 since this is a sync call) and do whatever is necessary to remember that this cancel scope belongs to this asyncgen. I just checked and ag_running appears to be True from the perspective of an async function awaited from the generator; it just flips back to False when the call stack actually traps to the event loop. So I don't think the async-ness of open_nursery() will get in the way either.

I'm sure the devil will be in the details, but can you see any missing pieces in the overall idea?

None of this helps with regular non-async generators that create a cancel scope. But since there's not much point to making a cancel scope that you can't put any awaits in, I don't think there's any use for such a generator besides decorating it with @contextmanager, and we've established we like the existing behavior in that case.

@ncoghlan Yeah, but my concern is that we won't be able to make the speed workable like that. Since this adds per-call and per-context-switch overhead it's *probably* worthwhile trying to get it as low as possible. But one reason for working through the thoughts here before taking something to bpo is to figure out exactly what semantics we'd want so we can prototype it with `set_coroutine_wrapper` and see what happens :-).

@loganbeal, I believe there is one way we may be able to get this working reliably and allow both naming patterns like `m.myVar` as well as `myVar` and support multiple models in one file. Let me explain and see what you think. 

First, we rename all the internal Gekko variables to the same name with a preceding underscore (`self.remote` becomes `self._remote`). This will allow us to prevent name collisions and keep people from overwriting our internal variables and methods. For our API surface names (`m.Var()`, `m.Equation()`) we leave them without underscores, but add these into a check when the variable is assigned and return an error if someone tries to overwrite one of our API surface names. This could be done somewhat like how we were trying to get the APM var names to be the same as the script names, but it should work.

Second, we use the internal `self._id` of the model a variable is generated from to "label" that variable as being for the given model. For example, if a variable is declared `myVar = m.Var()` and the model `m` has a `self._id` of 3, then `myVar._modelId = 3`.  

Finally, when the GUI checks the variables in the file it can filter them based on whether the `_modelId` property matches the model it is trying to collect data for. 

This was kind of long and could be somewhat involved, but I think it could resolve several of our problems and leave us with a more stable framework. What do you think?

I would prefer to see d(0,x) = 1 (regardless of whether there is normalization) to be compatible with sklearn. The interpretation to me is that the all-zero vector is orthogonal to x, which _can_ be true for many x. For d(0,x) = 2, the interpretation would be that 0 is the opposite of x, which _cannot_ be true for many x.

Then there's the choice of what d(0,0) should be. A reasonable option would be 0, but I can see 1 being reasonable as well (similar to how NaN != NaN). To break the tie, I would again go with what sklearn does, which is return 1.
